On 1 Jan 2005 20:20:33 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <qmedt0tk4qe3rqana1e5v9vse3psmeprf5@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>seen the movies, at least, I am sure.

I wouldn't get in front of covering fire in an offensive situation.
That's how friendly fire casualties occur. I did once, by accident. We
were attacking the enemy's flank (side) and covering firers didn't
know we were there. Only luck prevented us from getting killed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T15:56:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cr9n5d$np6$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <mjeft05kahl5kpmca3uh7p7a9ffkqhc9kj@4ax.com> <cr8o5m$q2t$1@panix5.panix.com> <b4cgt05njic81mktm2odg32939ruujfros@4ax.com>`

```
In article <b4cgt05njic81mktm2odg32939ruujfros@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 2 Jan 2005 07:07:18 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>make intelligent decisions. 

Special Ops are Marines, Mr Wagner, and Marines follow lawful orders... or 
so I have been told... and honorable men admit when their assertions have 
been disproven: if 'covering fire' counts then your assertions of 'The 
only firepower that counts is one bullet hitting one target. The ones that 
miss don't count.' have been disproven.

>
>Which are better suited to corporate Cobol programming?

That depends on whom one asks, Mr Wagner; from what I have seen, as posted 
in 
<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=345A9616.2612%40erols.com&output=gplain> 
and referenced in 
<http://groups.google.co.uk/groups?selm=ceo1hj%2411h%241%40panix5.panix.com&output=gplain> 
is that there are some who believe that the one best suited for their 
corporate COBOL programming slots would be a ''great, heads-down, 
run-with-the-ball coder who was a real Team Player'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-01-03T05:21:46-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<crb6bq$4vc$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <b4cgt05njic81mktm2odg32939ruujfros@4ax.com> <cr9n5d$np6$1@panix5.panix.com> <24bht0d0smt21rrbi48af7i8ri5tpbesvj@4ax.com>`

```
In article <24bht0d0smt21rrbi48af7i8ri5tpbesvj@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 2 Jan 2005 15:56:13 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
><Laugh> Yeah, right.

Yeah, right, Mr Wagner, this is what I have been told.  Are you calling me 
a liar?

>
>>and honorable men admit when their assertions have 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>offense.

Covering fire was presented as a tactic, Mr Wagner, and neither defensive 
or offensive was suggested; its use demonstrates that there are others 
than the bullet hitting the target which 'count'.

>The idea was to dissuade the enemy from attacking. 

The idea, Mr Wagner, was to pass along what might be knowledge; in 
demonstrating that there are situations where your original assertion is 
proven wrong it was hoped you might learn something new.

>
>>>Which are better suited to corporate Cobol programming?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>my experience, either homo- or hetero-.

You can ignore that part, Mr Wagner, and not lose too much of the import I 
intended... but it is a reference to an old joke told me by a temp 
secretary:

There are three candidates for a Executive Secretarial position.  One 
types 45 wpm and takes shorthand at 70 wpm (I told you it was an old 
joke), one types 55 wpm and takes shorthand at 85 wpm, one types 60 wpm 
and takes shorthand at 95.  Which one gets the job?

The one with the largest breasts... haw haw haw.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crebiu$707$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <qmedt0tk4qe3rqana1e5v9vse3psmeprf5@4ax.com> <cr7i91$g5o$1@panix5.panix.com> <mjeft05kahl5kpmca3uh7p7a9ffkqhc9kj@4ax.com> <cr8o5m$q2t$1@panix5.panix.com> <b4cgt05njic81mktm2odg32939ruujfros@4ax.com>`

```

On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> Infantry follows lawful orders; Special Ops are expected to think and
> make intelligent decisions.

Special Ops are expected to think, make intelligent decisions, and follow lawful
orders.    Good leaders give them orders that allow them to use their skills.  
They don't always have good leaders.

> Which are better suited to corporate Cobol programming?

CoBOL programmers are expected to think, make intelligent decisions, and follow
lawful orders.    Good managers give them orders that allow them to use their
skills.  They don't always have good managers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

In article <k0gbt01fqo8u9qahak9gdq3q43d2a3hrll@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> On 31 Dec 2004 15:51:02 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> ones that miss don't count.

The whole purpose of small arms fire is not to hit a target, but to 
limit it's movement so the squads heavy weapons, like mortars & machine 
guns, can engage it.

Bullets are far cheaper than paying out SGLI claims for people with John 
Wayne style tactics.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T19:07:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95ggt0pki121qm091u5ig83pmumnf1f2h7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <k0gbt01fqo8u9qahak9gdq3q43d2a3hrll@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C91ECD.18432501012005@knology.usenetserver.com> <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com> <cr8oe5$gjs$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 2 Jan 2005 07:11:49 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fish in a barrel, I tell ya.

Did you ever try to shoot fish in a barrel? It's trickier than it
seems.  Fish aren't where they appear to be because light refracts
when exiting water. Also, you can punch holes in the barrel, causing
victims to suffocate before you shoot them.

For more sportsmanlike entertainment, contact a wild game ranch such
as the YO in Texas, where they'll prop up a retired zoo animal for you
to murder, then mount its head as a keepsake. Imagine the fun you'll
have describing the experience to your grandchildren.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T16:00:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cr9ndn$5gq$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com> <cr8oe5$gjs$1@panix5.panix.com> <95ggt0pki121qm091u5ig83pmumnf1f2h7@4ax.com>`

```
In article <95ggt0pki121qm091u5ig83pmumnf1f2h7@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 2 Jan 2005 07:11:49 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>seems.

In the Air Force we were taught to use the proper tool for the job, Mr 
Wagner; while a torque-wrench can be used as a hammer, true, it might not 
be the most accurate torque-wrench afterwards.  Shooting fish in a barrel 
requires more energy and attention as using small quantities of low-grade 
explosives (things that used to be known as 'cherrybombs' or 'M-80s' a few 
decades back)...

... and *both* of those methods take time and equipment that simply 
pushing the barrel over and picking the things up do.

Now I *cannot* resist...

... try to think outside of the barrel, Mr Wagner... even though you might 
be more used to spending nights *in* them.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-03T02:38:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k6bht0hthhio6bg32q3edjr15agof52nfn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com> <cr8oe5$gjs$1@panix5.panix.com> <95ggt0pki121qm091u5ig83pmumnf1f2h7@4ax.com> <cr9ndn$5gq$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 2 Jan 2005 16:00:39 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <95ggt0pki121qm091u5ig83pmumnf1f2h7@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>In the Air Force we were taught to use the proper tool for the job

Air Force! That explains it all. 

>  Shooting fish in a barrel 
>requires more energy and attention as using small quantities of low-grade 
>explosives (things that used to be known as 'cherrybombs' or 'M-80s' a few 
>decades back)...

Rednecks do it with dynamite. Air Force personnel, being more
sophisticated(?), use July 4 fireworks. 

In the Marine Corps, we used one pound blocks of TNT to fell trees and
bridges. It never occurred to us to use explosives on fish.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-01-03T05:31:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crb6u1$bdm$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <95ggt0pki121qm091u5ig83pmumnf1f2h7@4ax.com> <cr9ndn$5gq$1@panix5.panix.com> <k6bht0hthhio6bg32q3edjr15agof52nfn@4ax.com>`

```
In article <k6bht0hthhio6bg32q3edjr15agof52nfn@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 2 Jan 2005 16:00:39 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Air Force! That explains it all. 

Even a Marine might be able to see through a wall, given enough time.

>
>>  Shooting fish in a barrel 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>sophisticated(?), use July 4 fireworks. 

Nothing was said about who used what, Mr Wagner, only about expenditures 
of energy... but 'rednecks'?  Smells like classism, Mr Wagner... you know, 
the kind of thing you decried in 
<http://groups.google.co.uk/groups?selm=afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p%404ax.com&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text

Classism via education. 'What do you expect from a high school
dropout.' Next you'll trot out the family pedigree. 

--end quoted text

So what comes next from you, Mr Wagner... taking pride in being able to do 
something you state 'everyone can do'?
 
>In the Marine Corps, we used one pound blocks of TNT to fell trees and
>bridges. It never occurred to us to use explosives on fish.

Isn't that interesting... now, Mr Wagner, even though you are a 
vegetarian... consider this new bit of piscatorial knowledge in light of 
the aphorism 'Give a man a fish and you feed him for a day, teach a man to 
fish and you feed him for life' and see what the difference in approaches 
might be between those who use something to destroy ('fell trees and 
bridges') and those who use the same thing to provide sustenance.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

```
On 3 Jan 2005 05:31:29 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <k6bht0hthhio6bg32q3edjr15agof52nfn@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>>Rednecks do it with dynamite
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>--end quoted text

The redneck appellation is based on *actions*, not education or
breeding.

>So what comes next from you, Mr Wagner... taking pride in being able to do 
>something you state 'everyone can do'?

Everyone could write good code, but many don't bother to learn how.
Everyone could knit a sweater, or juggle, or spin plates on sticks, or
hit a 150 mph backhand shot. It's all in technique.

>Isn't that interesting... now, Mr Wagner, even though you are a 
>vegetarian... consider this new bit of piscatorial knowledge in light of 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>bridges') and those who use the same thing to provide sustenance.

Thank you, sensei, for teaching me to kill fish with explosives. Will
the next lesson involve hitting a bothersome fly with a flame thrower?

Actually, we carried fishing line and hooks that weighed less than an
ounce (right tool for the job). After catching a medium sized fish,
we'd use it as bait to catch a seagull with the same line and hook.
Snakes were another easily caught food.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **Date:** 2005-01-04T01:19:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <k6bht0hthhio6bg32q3edjr15agof52nfn@4ax.com> <crb6u1$bdm$1@panix5.panix.com> <798jt09abgbf8kbpgiet80bh7apn92ociq@4ax.com> <crcm0j$fn3$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 3 Jan 2005 18:54:59 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <798jt09abgbf8kbpgiet80bh7apn92ociq@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>upstanding Colored Folks, right?

A few. Many more are trailer park rednecks. I disagree with their
views on diet, firearms, religion, hunting, etc. We agree on family
and joy of life. 

One of my ex-girlfriends called her three year-old 'sir' out of
respect for the nascent man within. I thought that was wonderful. My
mother never showed such respect.

>>>So what comes next from you, Mr Wagner... taking pride in being able to do 
>>>something you state 'everyone can do'?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>could.

I can write good code.

>>>Isn't that interesting... now, Mr Wagner, even though you are a 
>>>vegetarian... consider this new bit of piscatorial knowledge in light of 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>are capable of getting out of it then, for now, that will have to do.

Teaching implies superiority over students. I'd rather see mutual
respect, even between chronological unequals.

I'm now hosting two gentlemen aged 2 and 3 while their mother tries to
get her shit together in a shelter for abused women. The three
year-old shows psychological scars by refusing to be toilet trained
and by speaking baby-talk. Most foster parents would reject him. What
would you do with him? Take him to a professional, or nurture him as I
do?
```

>views on diet, firearms, religion, hunting, etc. We agree on family
>and joy of life. 

How very... large of them to allow you your opinions thusly.

>
>One of my ex-girlfriends called her three year-old 'sir' out of
>respect for the nascent man within. I thought that was wonderful. My
>mother never showed such respect.

Next time you might choose your parents better, Mr Wagner.

>
>>>>So what comes next from you, Mr Wagner... taking pride in being able to do 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>I can write good code.

Worthy of a style-manual, it has been said.

>
>>>>Isn't that interesting... now, Mr Wagner, even though you are a 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

condition for which I had hoped.

>
>I'm now hosting two gentlemen aged 2 and 3 while their mother tries to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>do?

I would not dream of asking someone to make an evaluation, Mr Wagner, nor

a few lines in a UseNet posting; to attempt to reduce the complexities of 
the lad's existence to such speaks *volumes* of you.

DD

On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >In the Air Force we were taught to use the proper tool for the job
>
> Air Force! That explains it all.

I was a USAF officer.   My brother is a Marine Officer.   In both cases,
officers do fighting.   In my era, there were *lots* of USAF officers who were
POWs because they were engaging the enemy directly.

You can disparage them the way you disparage programmers who believe in
following their boss's goals and objectives.   I won't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T10:37:07-05:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T19:07:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m8ggt096hge1c43olve58ikg889o44h0fm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3BCB88.10575831122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <u64bt094s02e2qcq0fg05n59i84a99d621@4ax.com> <cr4e3m$ab4$1@panix5.panix.com> <k0gbt01fqo8u9qahak9gdq3q43d2a3hrll@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C91ECD.18432501012005@knology.usenetserver.com> <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D83A4.10370702012005@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Sun, 02 Jan 2005 10:37:07 -0500, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com>,
> Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>costs more...

I remember taking that test .. as a 16 year-old high-school dropout at
Parris Island. I finished in half the time and sat there looking
around. A drill inspector asked what I was doing (don't know why his
comments were addressed to an insect larva). I said I was finished. He
said that's impossible, go back and check your answers. Not being the
polished diplomat I am today, I said "There's no reason to check them.
They're all right." Next thing I knew I was in the back of the room
doing pushups (having been promoted from larva to intelligent
posterior). While that was going on, the guy proctoring the exam
checked my answers, then tapped the DI on the shoulder and whispered
something, to which the DI responded "impossible". Anyway, I didn't
have to do more pushups.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T15:14:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crebvh$7ag$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3BCB88.10575831122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <u64bt094s02e2qcq0fg05n59i84a99d621@4ax.com> <cr4e3m$ab4$1@panix5.panix.com> <k0gbt01fqo8u9qahak9gdq3q43d2a3hrll@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C91ECD.18432501012005@knology.usenetserver.com> <2ffft0tanfb1p94v6obj3e90tr528sqakk@4ax.com>`

```

On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> Spoken like an Army guy. We Marines are proud of our marksmanship.

My brother is a Marine Colonel.    He knows that his job is to accomplish the
objectives of his superiors.   And he expects his subordinates to accomplish the
objectives he gives them.    The objectives aren't "hit a particular target",
but to "accomplish the mission".

There are people who put their personal objectives over the mission's
objectives.   These guys will show off their marksmanship, even if other tactics
are better for the team objectives.     These personality types are not limited
to military or sports, but can be found in programming staffs as well.   They
"know better" than their bosses and their bosses' missions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

wrote:

>On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>but to "accomplish the mission".

The mission of infantry is killing people and breaking things. The
units I was in had other missions, primarily information gathering.

>There are people who put their personal objectives over the mission's
>objectives.   These guys will show off their marksmanship, even if other tactics
>are better for the team objectives.     These personality types are not limited
>to military or sports, but can be found in programming staffs as well.   They
>"know better" than their bosses and their bosses' missions.

When the bosses' objectives change from day to day, or vary depending
on which boss you talk to, workers figure out there isn't a clearcut
objective.
```

On  4-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >> Spoken like an Army guy. We Marines are proud of our marksmanship.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The mission of infantry is killing people and breaking things.

You're confusing mission with method.    Marines have told me that their job is
to take an objective and then hand it over to the Army to keep it.  This Marine
method (not mission) involves similar killing and breaking to what you said the
infantry's mission is, while is Infantry method is not.

>The units I was in had other missions, primarily information gathering.

It's not obvious that marksmanship is directly related to the task of
information gathering.
```

Aftrthought:

The Kalashnikov family (e.g. AK-47) uses a low weight, high velocity
projectile similar to the M-15. Russian testers found they could
reliably hit targets at 500 meters, but couldn't penetrate winter
clothing.

cases where "good marksmanship" achieves the objectives best.   Apparently the
objectives are clear for "general use" - but not to me.

is to deny mobility to the other side.

In article <oqmlt0lhvu3isek4926r1q1gvqmku1b4vu@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> On Tue, 4 Jan 2005 15:03:44 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> is to deny mobility to the other side.

In terms of 'general infantry use' -- we had a position of 
auto-rifleman.  His sole job was to be first out of the APC or Bradley  
to 'hose down the area' towards the enemy, with a full 30-round 
magazine.  We kept M16A1's around for this purpose.

This distracted the enemy long enough for the rest of the squad to exit 
the vehicle and assume fighting positions.
```

> weapon. My objective was to sneak up on them and touch them with my
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

I mentioned this to a friend who is a bowhunter.

He said to have a reasonable chace of killing a deer with a hunting arrow
you need to be within 15 metres of it. Stalking skills to get that close to
a wild deer in the New Zealand forest have to be exceptional.The arrows cost
$40 each and most of them end up lost in the bush. About 1 target in 6 is
brought down and most of the carcasses are carried out of the bush and
eaten. "Incompetence" is not a word I would associate with this bloke, and
certainly not to his face... :-).

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T22:08:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<341p56F41kot0U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<cpat0e$n7$1@panix5.panix.com> <31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <cpb0qm$bus$1@panix5.panix.com> <31usn2F39796fU1@individual.net> <cpkdi4$lds$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <59pvd.7413$ZP5.7342@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <0mjdt05jpjgogvqpn86ckljvqk9imp0tck@4ax.com> <crecho$7km$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:crecho$7km$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Jan-2005, SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> How close does one have to be to turn from an animal murderer to a
sportsman?
>
That's a very fair question, Howard.

Personally I no longer hunt deer or pig, although I confess to having done
so in my youth. I certainly have never shot rabbits or possums "for fun"
even though both are considered noxious pests here.

I swerve for hedgehogs and any other small animals. On the rare occasions
when a bird flies into the car, I stop and check it is not just wounded. (I
always feel wretched about this because I enjoy driving and I don't like to
think of that enjoyment taking life...)

So to your question...

I believe it can only be "sport" when the animals have the same weapons I'm
using and they have freely chosen to participate.

Obviously, that is never going to be attainable, so you can argue (fairly)
that hunting (even fishing) is NOT sport.

However, I believe there are shades of grey here.

Clinically executing a deer at half a mile, with a telescopic sight on a
high powered rifle, is NOT the same thing as getting to within 15 metres of
the animal and attempting to shoot it with a bow and arrow.

Why not? (In both cases the desired outcome from the hunter's point of view
is a dead animal...)

Because, with the high technology solution the animal never had a chance.
With the low technology solution the odds are in favour of the prey
escaping, and there are other factors (like the acquired skill of the
huntsman) that may widen the gap even further. (A poorly skilled stalker
will NEVER get close enough to kill his prey, so his "hunting" is really the
same as if he'd left his bow at home and gone tramping... )

I therefore concede that while hunting and fishing may never be "sport" in
the true sense of the word, the more chance there is of the prey escaping,
the more "sporting" the endeavour can be said to be.

It is that "sporting chance" that changes it from animal murder to a test of
skill. (Sadly, the stakes in this test are very high for the animal, and
that is why many people choose not to particpate. Speaking only for myself,
I can say I have more respect for hunters who adopt the "sporting chance"
approach than I do for those who don't.)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

- **References:** `<cpat0e$n7$1@panix5.panix.com> <31scqsF3fjvp6U1@individual.net> <cpb0qm$bus$1@panix5.panix.com> <31usn2F39796fU1@individual.net> <cpkdi4$lds$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <59pvd.7413$ZP5.7342@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net>`

On Sat, 1 Jan 2005 21:48:08 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I mentioned this to a friend who is a bowhunter.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>eaten.

An arrow costs $40?! A dead deer costs $240! That's about $4 per pound
of edible meat. He could save money by taking pictures of the deer and
buying meat from a store.

I know people who hunt for subsistence rather than sport. They don't
use $40 arrows; they use a shotgun that fires a .62 cal slug at
subsonic speed. Contrary to popular belief, a gun's bang does not come
from exploding powder; it's a sonic boom caused by the projectile. A
subsonic projectile and inexpensive choke reduces the noise to 10
deci-bels (100 bels at 300 yards) to avoid the notice of game wardens.
The report is about the same as an air rifle.
```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 1 Jan 2005 21:48:08 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >you need to be within 15 metres of it. Stalking skills to get that close
to
> >a wild deer in the New Zealand forest have to be exceptional.The arrows
cost
> >$40 each and most of them end up lost in the bush. About 1 target in 6 is
> >brought down and most of the carcasses are carried out of the bush and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'll pass this wisdom on to him the next time I see him, Robert... :-)

Although venison IS available in stores, here it is usually farmed venison
and, as such, does not have the same flavour that the wild meat does.
Furthermore, the people who do this (much like the people who hunt wild pigs
with 3 dogs and a hunting knife) do it for the sport and the food. I don't
know how much gun ammunition you would buy for $NZ40, but these are
aluminium arrows with steel barbed hunting tips and match type flights. We
are not keen on guns here. Our cops don't normally carry them and don't
normally need them. The general population is not obsessed with guns and
very few households possess one. Handguns are, for the most part, illegal,
and rifles/shotguns require a licence. Automatic weapons are unobtainable
and you can't buy assault rifles, bazookas, or stinger missiles over the
counter... (I'd love to hear someone explaining why he needed a bazooka for
deer hunting...:-))

 Some people do hunt with rifles, but the number is declining. Outdoor
sports, including hunting and fishing are popular here. No licence is
required to hunt deer or pigs, and there is open season on them all year
round, as they were both introduced, and they both destroy the native
forest. I haven't checked GOOGLE, but I would think that our deaths per
annum from gunshot wounds, per head of population would be extremely low.

> I know people who hunt for subsistence rather than sport. They don't
> use $40 arrows; they use a shotgun that fires a .62 cal slug at
> subsonic speed.
Why?
Why not use a slingshot or crossbow? (I have friends here who hunt (edible)
birds with a slingshot.)

A specially modified gun to avoid game wardens seems a bit extreme to me...

> Contrary to popular belief, a gun's bang does not come
> from exploding powder; it's a sonic boom caused by the projectile.

Yes, the crack of a whip is caused by the tip of it passing through the
sound barrier.

>A
> subsonic projectile and inexpensive choke reduces the noise to 10
> deci-bels (100 bels at 300 yards) to avoid the notice of game wardens.
> The report is about the same as an air rifle.
>
Ah, but is it ""sport"...?

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<cpb0qm$bus$1@panix5.panix.com> <31usn2F39796fU1@individual.net> <cpkdi4$lds$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <59pvd.7413$ZP5.7342@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net>`

```
On Mon, 3 Jan 2005 00:52:00 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> I haven't checked GOOGLE, but I would think that our deaths per
>annum from gunshot wounds, per head of population would be extremely low.

Just about everywhere is lower than the US.

I was riding a bicycle in Irving, Texas, (a Dallas suburb) when a
pickup pulled alongside and the passenger shot at my head from five
feet away. Then they swerved across three lanes to attempt running
over a six year-old girl going the other way. Calling the police with
their license number was useless. A lawyer friend said I could sue in
civil court, but when I said they were juveniles (under 17 in Tx) he
said "I wish you'd told me that first. Forget it. You can't touch
juveniles."

I've been shot at from afar while bicycling on rural roads. I can
distinguish the sound of bullets coming toward me from shots in
another direction.

>> I know people who hunt for subsistence rather than sport. They don't
>> use $40 arrows; they use a shotgun that fires a .62 cal slug at
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>A specially modified gun to avoid game wardens seems a bit extreme to me...

A shotgun costs much less than a crossbow,  is useful for vermin and
birds as well as deer, and is excellent for self-defense. If a rural
person has only one weapon, it'll be a shotgun.

>> Contrary to popular belief, a gun's bang does not come
>> from exploding powder; it's a sonic boom caused by the projectile.
>
>Yes, the crack of a whip is caused by the tip of it passing through the
>sound barrier.

Another seldom mentioned fact is that a bullet breaks the sound
barrier twice. You can hear the second one if someone shoots directly
over your head from 300 yards. It sounds like a little firecracker
going off overhead.  The second shock wave deflects the bullet's
flight path. Subsonic rounds don't have this problem. At sea level
with normal humidity, the speed of sound is just over 1,000 feet per
second. If muzzle velocity is subsonic, the gun will not only be
quiet, it will also be more accurate for moderate distance, say 2-300
yards.

>Ah, but is it ""sport"...?

It's not intended to be. Shooting at bicyclers is sport.

The US Constitution says irresponsible rednecks can have guns because
they're members of the militia. A German friend put it this way: "You
Americans have guns; we have Autobahns. They're National religions.
Don't try to use logic against either."
```

- **References:** `<cpb0qm$bus$1@panix5.panix.com> <31usn2F39796fU1@individual.net> <cpkdi4$lds$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <59pvd.7413$ZP5.7342@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com>`

```

On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> The US Constitution says irresponsible rednecks can have guns because
> they're members of the militia

No.   The U.S. Constitution says: "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to
the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms,
shall not be infringed."

The English above is straightforward.

The Courts have found that exceptions are weapons particularly well suited for
Militias, and weapons particularly unsuited for Militias.
```

On Tue, 4 Jan 2005 15:30:06 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>The English above is straightforward.

It's not straightforward at all. Does it apply to militia members, or
people eligible for militia duty? Who does the regulating?

I used to work for a Texas supermarket chain that sold guns. Our
biggest seller, by far, was .25 cal handguns purchased by housewives.
How does "well regulated Militia" apply to that case?

When people are called to National Guard duty, they don't take their
own weapon.

>The Courts have found that exceptions are weapons particularly well suited for
>Militias, and weapons particularly unsuited for Militias.

One can legally own full-auto, silencers, sawed-off shotguns, etc. by
obtaining a BATF Class III permit, which costs $200. The going price
for an M-16 is about $20K. The weapon had to be manufactured before
1986 and access is subject to State law, as shown here:

http://www.westernfirearms.com/wfc/default?set=06
```

On  4-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >No.   The U.S. Constitution says: "A well regulated Militia, being necessary
> >to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> people eligible for militia duty? Who does the regulating?

The English says it applies to the people.

> Who does the regulating?

It doesn't say.    But that doesn't cloud what the words say.


> I used to work for a Texas supermarket chain that sold guns. Our
> biggest seller, by far, was .25 cal handguns purchased by housewives.
> How does "well regulated Militia" apply to that case?

Should it?   The English above just says that militia is necessary.   That could
be useful in defining "arms" to include what could be used by a militia.   The
courts have redefined it.   (Using English alone, we might argue that people
includes felons in prison).    I don't have any firearms, and don't want any -
but I do know how to read English.


> >The Courts have found that exceptions are weapons particularly well suited
> >for Militias, and weapons particularly unsuited for Militias.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1986 and access is subject to State law, as shown here:

You admit that this is limited.    Also, why should this amendment be subject to
State law?

- **References:** `<V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf1ba$k7o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Tue, 4 Jan 2005 21:19:36 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On  4-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>The English says it applies to the people.

Why didn't they write "The right of the people to keep and bear Arms
shall not be infringed?" It appears to me the militia clause is an
explanation -- BECAUSE a militia is necessary for security. In that
case, it doesn't apply to every individual, it means The People
collectively,  specifically those individuals who are  militia
members. Militia is defined in 10 USCS 311 as able-bodied men between
17 and 45 who are not members of the military.

The Second Amendment is based on a false premise. The militia has
never been called to serve, and probably never will be. It is NOT
"necessary to the security of a free State".

>> Who does the regulating?
>
>It doesn't say.    But that doesn't cloud what the words say.

States regulate the militia. The Constitution says so in the Tenth
Amendment.

>> I used to work for a Texas supermarket chain that sold guns. Our
>> biggest seller, by far, was .25 cal handguns purchased by housewives.
>> How does "well regulated Militia" apply to that case?
>
>Should it?   The English above just says that militia is necessary.  

Many things are necessary .. like air. One assumes there is a logical
connection between the two clauses in the Second Amendment. They
didn't include the first clause to fill space.

>That could
>be useful in defining "arms" to include what could be used by a militia.   

Logic will not save a failed argument. Face it, the Second Amendment
was a mistake.

>   I don't have any firearms, and don't want any - but I do know how to read English.

I don't have any either, but I went to law school.

>You admit that this is limited.    Also, why should this amendment be subject to
>State law?

Because the Feds aren't authorized to regulate firearms except in
interstate and international commerce.

They aren't authorized to regulate education, intrastate
transportation and public health either, but that's another
discussion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> The Second Amendment is based on a false premise. The militia has
> never been called to serve, and probably never will be. It is NOT
> "necessary to the security of a free State".

It's not necessary for the *defense* of a free State, but I'd say it's 
very necessary for the security of one.  How many dictatorships allow 
their average joe/non-connected citizens to own private firearms?  Were 
they always dictatorships?
```

On  4-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >> >No.   The U.S. Constitution says: "A well regulated Militia, being
> >> >necessary
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> shall not be infringed?"

They did.


> It appears to me the militia clause is an
> explanation -- BECAUSE a militia is necessary for security.

It appears that way to me too.   That's why "The right of the people to keep and
bear Arms shall not be infringed".


> In that
> case, it doesn't apply to every individual, it means The People
> collectively,  specifically those individuals who are  militia
> members. Militia is defined in 10 USCS 311 as able-bodied men between
> 17 and 45 who are not members of the military.

That's an assumption.    People who don't want to read it as written find this
to be a very attractive assumption.   I wouldn't mind seeing the amendment
repealed - but I am very much against reading the Constitution the way *I* want,
as others will read it the way *they* want.    Let's just read it the way it is.

A better argument is that this explanation for the amendment is that it defines
"arms" as weapons suited for use by a Militia.    But still - Let's read the
Constitution as it says, not as we wish it says.    We know our enemies are
willing to read it as they wish it says.


> Logic will not save a failed argument. Face it, the Second Amendment
> was a mistake.

I don't mind facing that.    Repeal it.    But don't pretend it says something
it doesn't.


> >You admit that this is limited.    Also, why should this amendment be subject
> >to State law?
>
> Because the Feds aren't authorized to regulate firearms except in
> interstate and international commerce.

You answered why this amendment should not be subject to federal law.   Is this
right to bear arms reserved to the states or to the people?    You seem to think
it's the states, I read the people.


> They aren't authorized to regulate education, intrastate
> transportation and public health either, but that's another
> discussion.

I'd rather have the 9th and 10th amendments enforced than the 2nd.    But my
preference is for our Great Leaders to accept an authority over their actions
without interpreting the words differently from what they say - to advance their
own power.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a4fot0d9bj8qi4l8a2qc7hagj3us49kitj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf1ba$k7o$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ig5mt0d97nf0krpd47p6v3n9asociejjj5@4ax.com> <crh0hn$m7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Wed, 5 Jan 2005 15:18:13 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On  4-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>as others will read it the way *they* want.    Let's just read it the way it is.

I am reading it as written. You want to brush aside the preamble,
interpreting the amendment as though the militia clause were deleted.

The US Supreme Court interpreted it my way. In US v. Miller it said:

"In the absence of any evidence tending to show that possession or use
of a 'shotgun having a barrel of less than eighteen inches in length'
at this time has some reasonable relationship to the preservation or
efficiency of a well regulated militia, we cannot say that the Second
Amendment guarantees the right to keep and bear such an instrument."

http://caselaw.lp.findlaw.com/scripts/getcase.pl?court=us&vol=307&invol=174

The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals offers this analysis:

" In the last few decades, courts and commentators have offered what
may fairly be characterized as three different basic interpretations
of the Second Amendment. The first is that the Second Amendment does
not apply to individuals; rather, it merely recognizes the right of a
state to arm its militia.(9) This "states' rights" or "collective
rights" interpretation of the Second Amendment has been embraced by
several of our sister circuits.(10) The government commended the
states' rights view of the Second Amendment to the district court,
urging that the Second Amendment does not apply to individual
citizens.

Proponents of the next model admit that the Second Amendment
recognizes some limited species of individual right. However, this
supposedly "individual" right to bear arms can only be exercised by
members of a functioning, organized state militia who bear the arms
while and as a part of actively participating in the organized
militia's activities. The "individual" right to keep arms only applies
to members of such a militia, and then only if the federal and state
governments fail to provide the firearms necessary for such militia
service. At present, virtually the only such organized and actively
functioning militia is the National Guard, and this has been the case
for many years. Currently, the federal government provides the
necessary implements of warfare, including firearms, to the National
Guard, and this likewise has long been the case. Thus, under this
model, the Second Amendment poses no obstacle to the wholesale
disarmament of the American people. A number of our sister circuits
have accepted this model, sometimes referred to by commentators as the
sophisticated collective rights model.(11) On appeal the government
has abandoned the states' rights model and now advocates the
sophisticated collective rights model.

The third model is simply that the Second Amendment recognizes the
right of individuals to keep and bear arms. This is the view advanced
by Emerson and adopted by the district court. None of our sister
circuits has subscribed to this model, known by commentators as the
individual rights model or the standard model. The individual rights
view has enjoyed considerable academic endorsement, especially in the
last two decades.(12) "

It then presents a well-reasoned and substantiated argument supporting
the third model. It's too long to post here. The conclusion:

"We have found no historical evidence that the Second
Amendment was intended to convey militia power to the states,
limit the federal government's power to maintain a standing
army, or applies only to members of a select militia while on
active duty.(60)  All of the evidence indicates that the Second
Amendment, like other parts of the Bill of Rights, applies to
and protects individual Americans."

http://caselaw.lp.findlaw.com/scripts/getcase.pl?court=5th&navby=case&no=9910331cr0

>A better argument is that this explanation for the amendment is that it defines
>"arms" as weapons suited for use by a Militia.    But still - Let's read the
>Constitution as it says, not as we wish it says.    We know our enemies are
>willing to read it as they wish it says.

Understanding what it MEANS, why it is there, involves history of the
Anti-Federalist movement. The Fifth Circuit (above) deals with that,
and also this analysis:

"The Revolutionary War taught, in other words, that a standing army is
necessary to effective military action. Accordingly, when it came time
to write the Constitution in 1787, in addition to empowering Congress
"to provide for calling forth the militia," the framers also gave
Congress the power "to raise and support armies" and "to provide and
maintain a navy." These latter forces were to be--and have
been--independent of state militias.

But if late eighteenth century Americans viewed a standing army as
necessary to national defense, they also saw it as, at best, a
necessary evil. Anti-Federalists and others who originally opposed
ratification of the Constitution worried, among other things, that
Congress would create a standing army that the President could use
oppressively.

The Second Amendment partly addressed this concern by prohibiting
Congressional abolition of the state militias. Continued existence of
state militias would mean both that Congress would have less need to
rely upon the standing army and that, should the standing army be used
as an agent of domestic oppression, the people could resist militarily
through their state militias."

http://writ.news.findlaw.com/scripts/printer_friendly.pl?page=/dorf/20030514.html

Is that fear still relevant? How likely is it that President Bush
would send the Army and Air Force  to invade Waco? If he did, do you
really think the Texas National Guard could defend it?

>> >You admit that this is limited.    Also, why should this amendment be subject
>> >to State law?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>it's the states, I read the people.

I didn't say States; I said militia. The Fifth Circuit argues that
the other amendments 1-8 apply to individuals.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T13:56:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<59pvd.7413$ZP5.7342@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com>`

```
Just to throw some gasoline on the fire with respect to the Constitutional
citation:

However I might feel about gun control (or the lack thereof) on personal,
moral or ethical grounds -- and I'm inclined more in favor of it than
against it -- I have come to the conclusion that private citizens originally
were permitted to own just about the most powerful weaponry available to the
military, and that the arguments that the "right to bear arms" has never
applied beyond the formally-organized militia is at the very least weak.

I believe the principle is founded in Article 1 Section 8 Clause 11 of the
constitution, and was arguably most visibly emphasized during the War of
1812.  In that latter war, the navy had 23 armed ships with a total of 556
guns, while 517 privateers were licensed, and the armament of their ships
totalled 2,893 guns.

Privateers figured significantly in the Revolution, the War of 1812, the War
for Texas Independence, the Mexican War, and (most particularly on the
Confederate side) in the Civil War.  While Europe repudiated privateering in
the Declaration of Paris in 1856, the US apparently refused to do so until
it finally signed the Hague Convention of 1907.

Best I can tell, the privateer ship of th early 19th century was not
significantly different in power or character from the larger and more
powerful armed military vessels of the day.   In fact, it's pretty clear the
US relied *primarily* on privaters most particularly in the War of 1812.

Because of the constitutional principles and the way those principles were
implemented over the first century of our nation's history, I am inclined to
conclude that it might be a violation of fundamental constitutional rights
for a citizen to be prohibited from owning a medium-sized aircraft carrier
complete with all its aircraft, a fully-armed Polaris submarine of a
generation or so back, or a fully-armed B-52 bomber, because such vessels
are *relatively* in the same power ball-park as the best the military has
available, and *historically* citizens were allowed to own devices of
relatively-similar power.

And even if such licensing for use on behalf of the country is now
prohibited by international treaty, the principles, *and* the precedent of
the early history of the US, *both* presuppos the ability to *possess* them.

Again, that begs the question as to whether I think it *ought to be* legal
for the wacko down the street to own a B-52, an Abrams tank, an AK-47, a
bolt-action .22 rifle, a BB-gun or even a toy slingshot,  but I think the
argument is sound that, as far as the US constitution goes, restrictions on
private weaponry ownership -- including that weaponry that *governments* are
prohibited by treaty from using, and those that only governments are
*likely* to have the resources to purchase or use -- are decidedly
questionable.

    -Chuck Stevens


"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 4 Jan 2005 15:30:06 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >No.   The U.S. Constitution says: "A well regulated Militia, being
necessary to
> >the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear
Arms,
> >shall not be infringed."
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >The Courts have found that exceptions are weapons particularly well
suited for
> >Militias, and weapons particularly unsuited for Militias.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

On Tue, 4 Jan 2005 13:56:49 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Just to throw some gasoline on the fire with respect to the Constitutional
>citation:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>totalled 2,893 guns.

Article 1:8:11 says Congress has the authority to "Issue letters of
marquee and reprisal". Translated: hire mercinaries.  More relevent
are clauses 15 and 16:

Clause 15: To provide for calling forth the Militia to execute the
Laws of the Union, suppress Insurrections and repel Invasions;

Clause 16: To provide for organizing, arming, and disciplining, the
Militia, and for governing such Part of them as may be employed in the
Service of the United States, reserving to the States respectively,
the Appointment of the Officers, and the Authority of training the
Militia according to the discipline prescribed by Congress; 

Congress has never exercised clause 15, has never called forth the
Militia. Granted, it hired mercinaries, who today would be called
independent contractors. In my experience with them, the Soldier of
Fortune types are dreadful failures. They're long on bravado, short on
winning performance.

>Privateers figured significantly in the Revolution, the War of 1812, the War
>for Texas Independence, the Mexican War, and (most particularly on the
>Confederate side) in the Civil War.  While Europe repudiated privateering in
>the Declaration of Paris in 1856, the US apparently refused to do so until
>it finally signed the Hague Convention of 1907.

All true. 

>And even if such licensing for use on behalf of the country is now
>prohibited by international treaty, the principles, *and* the precedent of
>the early history of the US, *both* presuppose the ability to *possess* them.

No, it doesn't. The Hague Convention says nothing about that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T10:00:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com...

> Article 1:8:11 says Congress has the authority to "Issue letters of
> marquee and reprisal". Translated: hire mercinaries.  More relevent
> are clauses 15 and 16:

The term "letter of marquee and reprisal" appears to have been used, both
before and after the American Revolution, as the authority to act as a
privateer on behalf of another government.  I do not believe its primary
*focus* was the hiring of mercenaries, and I believe strongly that the
historical record is clear that such letters -- so named -- did indeed
detail the authority granted to privateers.    It is the constitutional
basis for the hiring of the equipment as well as the crew of *private*
entrepreneurs who had the wherewithal to *own* ships that rivaled, or even
exceeded, the best the navy of the time had to offer in terms of speed,
agility and firepower.

But the point I am making is not about calling forth militia, it is about
the *sorts* of weapons that a private citizen is *constitutionally presumed*
to be able to own.  If Congress has the authority to contract with private
battleship owners to help with the Navy's work -- and not even under Navy
command -- then it must be presumed that the set of "private battleship
owners" is something other than the null set according to the constitution.

Clauses 15 and 16 are irrelevant to that point.

> >And even if such licensing for use on behalf of the country is now
> >prohibited by international treaty, the principles, *and* the precedent
of
> >the early history of the US, *both* presuppose the ability to *possess*
them.
>
> No, it doesn't. The Hague Convention says nothing about that.

Not sure which "it" you're talking about, but I think you're wrong on both
possible points.

The US was a signator to the Hague Convention of 1907.  Section VII Article
1 requires that merchantmen used as or converted into warships must be under
the command of the "Power under whose flag it flies".   Such was not the
case for privateers, who were free to do whatever their captains thought was
appropriate on behalf of the country without answering directly to the
military or the government.  That's precisely the difference between a
privateer (essentially a licensed pirate) and a merchantman converted to
military service.


    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T21:22:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 5 Jan 2005 10:00:31 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>agility and firepower.

A warship for hire sounds to me like a mercenary. Nowadays most
mercenaries are foot soldiers. I've read there are 20,000 of them in
Iraq (on our side). The Bay of Pigs invasion used a private army, navy
and air force. Nobody knows who owned them because they weren't flying
a flag. Like most mercenary operations, it was a model of ineptitude.
Planes kept getting lost, ships ran aground, infantry ran out of
ammunition. 

>But the point I am making is not about calling forth militia, it is about
>the *sorts* of weapons that a private citizen is *constitutionally presumed*
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>owners" is something other than the null set according to the constitution.

All articles in the Bill of Rights are subject to reasonable
limitations. The right to free speech doesn't legalize slander. Double
jeopardy doesn't preclude a second trial when the jury cannot agree.
Freedom from self-incrimination doesn't bar the defendant from
testifying. The right to bear arms can be limited to adults who are
sane, and can exclude criminal-style weapons such as sawed-off
shotguns.

>> >And even if such licensing for use on behalf of the country is now
>> >prohibited by international treaty, the principles, *and* the precedent
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>military service.

Economics precludes private battleships and fighter planes. "It"
referred to possession of military-style guns. It IS legal to possess
exotic guns, provided you pay a BATF tax and convince a local official
to sign off. David Koresh was a licensed dealer of such weapons; look
what happened to him.

A former neighbor had a store that sold exclusively full-auto weapons
and other military/survivalist gear. He went broke because there
weren't enough 'patriots' in West Texas.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-06T15:52:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com...

> A warship for hire sounds to me like a mercenary.

Privateers aren't "warships for hire".  They get *permission* (via a "letter
of marque and reprisal") from a government to be what would otherwise be
pirates and to do what would otherwise be regarded as piracy.  They are
motivated by what they are *permitted by the issuing government to steal*.

A mercenary is *hired*, is by definition *paid* by the hiring *army*, and is
almost always *not* a citizen of the hiring country.


> Nowadays most
> mercenaries are foot soldiers. I've read there are 20,000 of them in
> Iraq (on our side).

Irrelevant, unless each and every one of those footsoldiers hired into an
army has *also* received a "letter of marque and reprisal" from the US
congress permitting them to seize for their own use the assets of other
individuals or agencies in Iraq at will!  I don't think so!

> The Bay of Pigs invasion used a private army, navy
> and air force.

That doesn't make them privateers.   That doesn't mean they were covered
under the subject article of the Constitution, either.

> Nobody knows who owned them because they weren't flying
> a flag. Like most mercenary operations, it was a model of ineptitude.
> Planes kept getting lost, ships ran aground, infantry ran out of
> ammunition.

And this relates to "privateer" and "mercenary" being covered by exactly the
same set of statutes (constitutional and otherwise) ... exactly how?


> All articles in the Bill of Rights are subject to reasonable
> limitations.

"Reasonable" is in the eye of the beholder, and is subject to modification.
In 1812 it would have been perfectly legal, even admirable, for me to own a
full-scale battleship if I so desired, and go off raiding British
merchantmen and blowing away whatever British military vessels I ran across
at will, by the simple expedient of getting Congress to grant me a Letter of
Marque and Reprisal.

> The right to free speech doesn't legalize slander.

Isn't that primarily a state/local civil issue rather than a matter of
criminal law?

> Double
> jeopardy doesn't preclude a second trial when the jury cannot agree.

I think this is implicit in the wording "be subject to the same offense to
be twice put in jeopardy of life or limb", myself.

> Freedom from self-incrimination doesn't bar the defendant from
> testifying.

Reread the fifth amendment; there's nothing in the amendment itself that
says you *can't* incriminate yourself, only that you can't be *compelled* to
do so as a witness.

> The right to bear arms can be limited to adults who are
> sane,

Just as "reasonable" is in the eye of the beholder, so is "sane".

> and can exclude criminal-style weapons such as sawed-off
> shotguns.

Well, my point is that until about a hundred years ago it *didn't* exclude
cannon or battleships!

> Economics precludes private battleships and fighter planes.

Probably only because of income and estate taxes!

> "It"
> referred to possession of military-style guns.

Which wasn't the topic of discussion of the paragraph you were citing.  The
use of *privateers* was, and the relationship of a privateer ship to the
military weaponry of the day.

> It IS legal to possess
> exotic guns, provided you pay a BATF tax and convince a local official
> to sign off. David Koresh was a licensed dealer of such weapons; look
> what happened to him.

Irrelevant to my point.  Is it *legal* to own a working Abrams tank?  A
Polaris submarine?  An aircraft carrier?   A nuclear weapon?

> A former neighbor had a store that sold exclusively full-auto weapons
> and other military/survivalist gear. He went broke because there
> weren't enough 'patriots' in West Texas.

Anybody who had the funds in the first half of the 19th century could build
the biggest, baddest armed ocean-going vessel he could envision, without any
sort of license at all.  If the US went to war, or even got piqued at some
government, the owner could get a "letter of marque and reprisal" from the
US congress and go off on raiding parties against that government, and would
keep whatever goods he might capture in that process.

The fact that doing so today is not *economically* feasible is irrelevant to
my point that it is now considered *illegal* as well as impractical.  I
believe any *law* that prevents me -- or you -- from owning something like
an armed ICBM, by the precedent of both the *allowance* of massive private
weaponry and its semi-official use in several wars in the form of
privateering, is in direct violation of the Second Amendment.

I'd also note that the US *chose* to abide by the 1856 Declaration of Paris
and to eschew the use of privateers during the Spanish-American War of 1898,
just because we were Nice Guys (and I think because we wanted to preserve at
least the appearance of Righteous Indignation over the explosion of the
Battleship Maine and retain the Moral High Ground (IMHO not that we
succeeded in that respect!).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **References:** `<33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

On Thu, 6 Jan 2005 15:52:07 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>individuals or agencies in Iraq at will!  I don't think so!

Marque and reprisal applies to naval operations, not foot soldiers.

>> The right to free speech doesn't legalize slander.
>
>Isn't that primarily a state/local civil issue rather than a matter of
>criminal law?

The Bill of Rights is not limited to criminal issues. The right to
trial by jury applies to civil cases.

>> The right to bear arms can be limited to adults who are
>> sane,
>
>Just as "reasonable" is in the eye of the beholder, so is "sane".

That decision is made by medical doctors.

>> and can exclude criminal-style weapons such as sawed-off
>> shotguns.
>
>Well, my point is that until about a hundred years ago it *didn't* exclude
>cannon or battleships!

When possession of a specific weapon poses more of a danger to the
public than a safeguard, banning that weapon is a reasonable
exception. Even ardent gun supporters agree when it comes to nukes and
anti-aircraft missles. 

>> Economics precludes private battleships and fighter planes.
>
>Probably only because of income and estate taxes!

Income taxes are illegal too .. according to some. Here's what the IRS
says:

http://www.irs.gov/compliance/enforcement/article/0,,id=106444,00.html

>> "It"
>> referred to possession of military-style guns.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>military weaponry of the day.

I don't see a connection between privateers and the Second Amendment.
The Second Amendment is about ground-pounding militia. Its weapons
were described in the Militia Act of 1792, where they do include
artillery.

http://www.constitution.org/mil/mil_act_1792.htm

>> It IS legal to possess
>> exotic guns, provided you pay a BATF tax and convince a local official
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Polaris submarine?  An aircraft carrier?   A nuclear weapon?

An Abrams tank is an appropriate militia weapon; the others are not.
It's legal to own a tank, but not the howitzer.

>Anybody who had the funds in the first half of the 19th century could build
>the biggest, baddest armed ocean-going vessel he could envision, without any
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>privateering, is in direct violation of the Second Amendment.

 Weapons with a bore larger than half an inch were banned in 1934, 26
USC 53.5845. The justification at the time was stopping rum runners
and bank robbers. The act also bans grenades, bombs and rockets.

http://assembler.law.cornell.edu/uscode/html/uscode26/usc_sec_26_00005845----000-.html
```

- **References:** `<33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com> <crmnug$l5l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Fri, 7 Jan 2005 19:28:10 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On  7-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Are there legal criteria for sanity?

Before the Mï¿½Naghten decision in 1843, the standard was 'wild beast'.
After M'Naghten, the House of Lords defined it as:

ï¿½To establish a defense on the ground of insanity, it must clearly be
proved that, at the time of the committing of the act, the party
accused was laboring under such a defect of reason, from disease of
the mind, as not to know the nature and quality of the act he  was
doing; or if he did know it, that he did not know he was doing was
wrongï¿½ 

>> When possession of a specific weapon poses more of a danger to the
>> public than a safeguard, banning that weapon is a reasonable
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>objective way of measuring the value of such a danger and of such a safeguard.

The standard is popular opinion .. of the man on the street. 

I didn't think there was anything wrong with my neighbor in
Parsippany, NJ, owning a 300 mW laser pointer, until he pointed it at
a plane approaching Teterboro and blinded the pilot. The FBI descended
on him like you wouldn't believe. He's now facing 25 years in jail for
playing with a $130 yuppie toy.
