###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-07T12:35:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crmrqe$19pe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com...

> Marque and reprisal applies to naval operations, not foot soldiers.

I'm having some trouble establishing a clear consistency between the above
and your posting of 1/4/2005 which says in part  "Article 1:8:11 says
Congress has the authority to "Issue letters of
marquee and reprisal". Translated: hire mercinaries. "

Could you clarify for me how these two statements represent a single
perspective?   Or have you changed your mind?

> >> The right to free speech doesn't legalize slander.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> trial by jury applies to civil cases.

You missed the point.  The details of what is and what is not slander are
state and local issues; so far as I have been able to determine, the only
*federal* laws I know of are those that prohibit suing the federal
government for libel or slander.

> That decision is made by medical doctors.

Who gets to decide which medical doctors?   And is the gentleman down the
street wearing a tinfoil hat so that the FBI can't read his thoughts insane
or a righteous patriot behaving reasonably?  Wouldn't that depend on the
personal views of the *doctor*?

> >Well, my point is that until about a hundred years ago it *didn't*
exclude
> >cannon or battleships!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> anti-aircraft missles.

It wasn't a "reasonable exception" 100 years ago!   That's *exactly* my
point!  What ardent gun supporters -- or for that matter anti-gun
ctivists  -- agree or disagree upon today is not all that relevant to what
rights the constitution explicitly or implicitly granted, and what the
Founding Fathers thought was appropriate in 1789 (or before) and embodied in
the Constitution.  .

It was considered *reasonable* for a private citizen during the War of 1812
to own a ship that could literally blow the best the British -- or the US -- 
Navy had to offer right smack out of the water, and to do a pretty good job
of smashing a good-sized harbor-defense fort to smithereens had the captain
a mind to do so!

Even as the US signed the 1907 Hague Declaration, it didn't agree to
*prohibit* citizens from owning ships that could be used as privateers, it
just agreed not to issue Letters of Marque and Reprisal that would allow
them to engage in piracy legally!

> >> Economics precludes private battleships and fighter planes.
> >
> >Probably only because of income and estate taxes!
>
> Income taxes are illegal too .. according to some.

That's one reason I mentioned it!   But it's probably *estate* taxes that
are the bigger issue.

> I don't see a connection between privateers and the Second Amendment.

Only if one takes the position that the Second Amendment *limits* the sort
of weaponry a private citizen can own.  I think it's a *clarification* of
that point already established in Article 1 Section 8 Item 11.

> The Second Amendment is about ground-pounding militia. Its weapons
> were described in the Militia Act of 1792, where they do include
> artillery.

Irrelevant to the point as to what weaponry the Constitution allows -- or
may be shown to presume to allow -- private citizens to own *outside* of
what's applicable to the Second Amendment.

> An Abrams tank is an appropriate militia weapon; the others
> [Polaris submarine, aircraft carrier, nuclear weapon]
> are not.It's legal to own a tank, but not the howitzer.

If the presumption that a private ship could constitutiontally be armed with
as many nine-pound cannon as the builder wished, why is it unreasonable to
assume that a land-based vehicle could be armed the same way?   A
fully-armed brigantine wasn't an appropriate *militia* weapon in 1812,
either!  Was owning one *unconstitutional* or *illegal* because of the
Militia Act of 1792?

>  Weapons with a bore larger than half an inch were banned in 1934, 26
> USC 53.5845. The justification at the time was stopping rum runners
> and bank robbers. The act also bans grenades, bombs and rockets.

Yeah, I'm not surprised.  I'm also not at all convinced it's constitutional.
It was constitutional, legal, and even patriotic to own a full warship in
1812, and I would argue that a law that does *not* amend the constitution
but *does* prohibit such ownership is of at best questionable
constitutionality.

I do not ascribe the status of Holy Writ to the constitution, nor do I
ascribe either Divine Wisdom or Divine Foresight to its framers.   Article 1
Section 8 Item 11, along with Amendment II, might have been meaningful back
in 1789, but application of either one *as they were almost certainly
envisioned by the framers* is not reasonable today.

Note that I do not own anything more lethal than a hunting knife, nor do I
have a particular desire to do so.  I have no particular animosity toward
those who do have more deadly weaponry, so long as they don't use it against
me.  I have no personal vested interest on either side of the argument as to
what sort of weaponry a private citizen "ought to be able" to own, whether
on constitutional grounds or otherwise.

As I see it, the framers of the Constitution would almost certainly allow
today's tinfoil-hat anti-everything paranoid wacko to own whatever weaponry
he or she wants to own; if his behavior got even *more* bizarre, he might be
locked up and not have *access* to his property.  And that's the way I think
they wrote the Constitution.  Whether that's a Good Thing or a Bad Thing I
don't really care, but I do think that's the way it *is*.   Do I think an
amendment repealing either of these Constitutional amendments would stand a
prayer of passage?  Nope.   Do I think laws that limit the weaponry one
might own are unconstitutional?  Yup.  Am I interested enough to file suit
to challenge them?  Nope.  Do I think such laws are appropriate?  Yeah,
probably.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-08T07:20:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com> <crmrqe$19pe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 7 Jan 2005 12:35:44 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>perspective?   Or have you changed your mind?

I see no connection between the Second Amendment and 1:8:11. The
Second Amendment is about States' Rights and people's rights against a
Federal government. The privateer thing is about defending the
Federation from assault by other countries.

>> That decision is made by medical doctors.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>personal views of the *doctor*?

We use board-certified psychologists. Sure it's fallible. What to you
propose as a better test of sanity?

>> >Well, my point is that until about a hundred years ago it *didn't*
>exclude
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>the Constitution.

Things change with time. It's no longer 1789. In Cobol terms, it's no
longer 1959. When the 'founding fathers' of Cobol gave us ALTER, they
were simply mistaken.

>Even as the US signed the 1907 Hague Declaration, it didn't agree to
>*prohibit* citizens from owning ships that could be used as privateers, it
>just agreed not to issue Letters of Marque and Reprisal that would allow
>them to engage in piracy legally!

If no country would employ them, privateers were effectively outlawed.

>> I don't see a connection between privateers and the Second Amendment.
>
>Only if one takes the position that the Second Amendment *limits* the sort
>of weaponry a private citizen can own.  I think it's a *clarification* of
>that point already established in Article 1 Section 8 Item 11.

I disagree. The Second Amendment gives rights to individual citizens.
1:8:11 gives rights to the federal government.

>If the presumption that a private ship could constitutiontally be armed with
>as many nine-pound cannon as the builder wished, why is it unreasonable to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Militia Act of 1792?

The Constitution doesn't say whether individuals may legally own a
brigantine. It does say, in the Ninth Amendment, that rights not
explicitly granted to the federal government are retained by the
people. Because 1:8:11 authorizes the federal government to employ
brigantines, one could interpret that as a transfer of rights from
individuals to government.

>>  Weapons with a bore larger than half an inch were banned in 1934, 26
>> USC 53.5845. The justification at the time was stopping rum runners
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>constitutionality.

You seem attached to flamboyant pirates. Did you see Johnny Depp's
impersonation of Keith Richards in Pirates of the Caribbean?

>I do not ascribe the status of Holy Writ to the constitution, nor do I
>ascribe either Divine Wisdom or Divine Foresight to its framers.   Article 1
>Section 8 Item 11, along with Amendment II, might have been meaningful back
>in 1789, but application of either one *as they were almost certainly
>envisioned by the framers* is not reasonable today.

Amen, brother. Level heads have prevailed since 1789, despite the
Constitution's presumtion of infallibility.

>Note that I do not own anything more lethal than a hunting knife, nor do I
>have a particular desire to do so. 

You should consider Kyocera's high-tech ceramic knives. They're as
hard as a diamond, never require sharpening, you can shave with them.
They're a delight in the kitchen. They look like cheap plastic but
cost over $100.

>As I see it, the framers of the Constitution would almost certainly allow
>today's tinfoil-hat anti-everything paranoid wacko to own whatever weaponry
>he or she wants to own; if his behavior got even *more* bizarre, he might be
>locked up and not have *access* to his property.  And that's the way I think
>they wrote the Constitution. 

Actually, no. The concept of insanity was poorly formed in 1789. They
thought the insane had no more logic than wild animals. More recent
decisions dealt with those who use ultraviolet light on doorknobs and
toilet handles.
```

```
Check the stylish EMF-shielding clothing at this legit site. I
especially like the bed canopy (too bad they didn't shield the ground
plane) and the dress shirt. They also have shields for cordless
phones.  The danger of cellphone radiation should be obvious. After
talking to a headhunter on one, my mind reels for an hour.

Bedfordshire (quoted because I adore English place names), who was
forced to shield his entire house.

A few years ago, 60 Minutes ran an article on high-voltage power lines
killing cattle. It appeared to be capacitive coupling between power
lines and metal fences and buildings. That story was buried  by
government/industry, as usual. But here is scientific proof that the
Russians were working on killing us with low-frequency waves, and did
cause widespread cattle deaths in Oregon. (Oregon is known for a
spectacularly beautiful coastline and mundanely ugly towns along it.
New England, by contrast, has a so-so coastline with beautiful towns
along it. The Atlantic coast of Florida is good in both.)
http://groups.msn.com/Tyrannicidae/frequenciescankillhealtransend.msnw
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Ultra-violet light on doorknobs and toilet handles was the science and religion thread

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2005-01-10T14:33:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cru3ns09b@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com> <crmrqe$19pe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com> <34aoh6F495u40U1@uni-berlin.de> <o8s3u051ac99s40vnqcl7ao9k9f25gpipm@4ax.com> <1105334466.653989.278420@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

In article <1105334466.653989.278420@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes:
> > If they didn't work, the government wouldn't have granted a patent.
> 
> That is incredibly naive, even for you.  The patent office has no idea
> whether it could work or not, nor has any interest in whether it would
> or not.

I think Robert intended that as sarcasm.  The entire post was, I
believe, a suggestion that Clark's reference to contemporary
sanitation mania does not justify (though it may explain) the
practice of attempting to sanitize doorknobs with UV light.

I'm with Robert on this one, frankly.  Bacteria are everywhere, and
sanitizing doorknobs will not make a bit of difference (aside from
perhaps improving the evolutionary chances of bacteria resistant to
the sanitation method being employed).  People would do just as well
to wipe down their homes with dollar bills as with those "sanitizing"
wipes and the like.

It's nice to keep things clean.  It's good to avoid gross contamin-
ation, whether that's washing hands or keeping raw meat away from
prepared food or what have you.  But trying to disinfect surfaces in
the home is a fool's errand.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 7 Jan 2005 12:35:44 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I'm having some trouble establishing a clear consistency between the
above
> >and your posting of 1/4/2005 which says in part  "Article 1:8:11 says
> >Congress has the authority to "Issue letters of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Federation from assault by other countries.

I think you missed my point.   I can't reconcile "Marque and reprisal
applies to naval operations [privateers], not foot soldiers [mercenaries]"
with "Article 1.8.11 ... 'Marquee and Reprisal':  translated:  hire
mercenaries."

> >> >Well, my point is that until about a hundred years ago it *didn't*
> >exclude
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >activists  -- agree or disagree upon today is not all that relevant to
what
> >rights the constitution explicitly or implicitly granted, and what the
> >Founding Fathers thought was appropriate in 1789 (or before) and embodied
in
> >the Constitution.
>
> Things change with time. It's no longer 1789.

Then how are the views of the Framers of that constitution relevant *today*?

> >Even as the US signed the 1907 Hague Declaration, it didn't agree to
> >*prohibit* citizens from owning ships that could be used as privateers,
it
> >just agreed not to issue Letters of Marque and Reprisal that would allow
> >them to engage in piracy legally!
>
> If no country would employ them, privateers were effectively outlawed.

No.  If no country *chose* to issue the appropriate documentation at a given
time (whether the country was a signator to the Declaration or not), the
issue is simply rendered moot.

> I disagree. The Second Amendment gives rights to individual citizens.
> 1:8:11 gives rights to the federal government.

I guess you *still* don't see it.  Article 1.8.11 *presupposes* the right of
a private citizen (or a foreigner, for that matter) to own any armament they
want; the right it explicitly *gives* is the right of Congress to allow such
a citizen to use that armament at will against another nation or party.
Thirty cannon is thirty cannon whether they be mounted together on a mobile
platform or individually mounted on carriages, regarded as part of a single
weapon or taken individually.

why is it unreasonable to
> >assume that a land-based vehicle could be armed the same way?   A
> >fully-armed brigantine wasn't an appropriate *militia* weapon in 1812,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> brigantine.

What relates to the topic is that it allows Congress to grant explicit
permission and protection to individuals who own brigantines so that they
can go beyond US borders and capture or destroy other ships or installations
with them.  The authority of Congress to grant these Letters requires that
there be indivuduals to whom the letters are addressed for whom the letters
are meaningful.

> It does say, in the Ninth Amendment, that rights not
> explicitly granted to the federal government are retained by the
> people. Because 1:8:11 authorizes the federal government to employ

Nope.  Not *employ*.

>
> brigantines, one could interpret that as a transfer of rights from
> individuals to government.

One could interpret it as a Bach fugue or a rutabaga, I suppose.

 > You seem attached to flamboyant pirates.

Why would you suggest that?   I'm hard-pressed to distinguish between a
privateer and a pirate ship; if you're being attacked by either one, the
distinction is moot!

> Did you see Johnny Depp's
> impersonation of Keith Richards in Pirates of the Caribbean?

I saw Johnny Depp's performance in that film, but I wouldn't know Keith
Richards from a clerk at McDonald's, so your question doesn't mean a lot to
me.  I gather he's some sort of musician, but I haven't run into mentions of
him as a superior interpreter of the music that is more likely to interest
me, and I don't think I've heard any of the conservatory-trained musicians I
know mention him (positively, negatively or otherwise) offhand ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-10T23:47:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com> <crmrqe$19pe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com> <crupc5$ifd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Mon, 10 Jan 2005 12:43:09 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >perspective?   Or have you changed your mind?

Yes, I have changed my mind. On 1/4, I thought Letters of Marque
applied to any mercenary -- naval or land-based. Having since done a
little research at
http://www.geocities.com/Tokyo/Garden/5213/marque.htm 
and 
http://www.geocities.com/Tokyo/Garden/5213/marque2.htm
I came to realize they applied only to naval mercenaries called
privateers. 

Letters of Marque are now of historic interest only. The US stopped
using them in the 19th century, after signing the Declaration of
Paris.

>> I see no connection between the Second Amendment and 1:8:11. The
>> Second Amendment is about States' Rights and people's rights against a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>mercenaries."

Same as above.

>> >It wasn't a "reasonable exception" 100 years ago!   That's *exactly* my
>> >point!  What ardent gun supporters -- or for that matter anti-gun
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Then how are the views of the Framers of that constitution relevant *today*?

Their views on principles remain valid. Their writing about technology
is obsolete. 

Letters of Marque are clearly obsolete. So is the notion of State
Militia to protect us from federal standing armies -- they've never
been employed. 

So where does that leave the people, with their arms? Given a
Constitution that's very difficult to amend, it leaves them in limbo
.. buffeted by court decisions on one side and administrative
bureaucrats on the other. The Constitution is not a solid island of
surety that many long for.

>> I disagree. The Second Amendment gives rights to individual citizens.
>> 1:8:11 gives rights to the federal government.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>weapon or taken individually.

No, I still don't see it. Article 1.8.11 doesn't grant any rights to
US citizens. It assumes privateers will be available, primarily from
other countries or no country, without giving Americans permission to
be privateers. The only permission it grants, in the Second Amendment,
is referred to in Article 1.8.12.

It would not be appropriate to issue a Letter of Marque to a private
army armed with thirty carriage-mounted guns.

>> The Constitution doesn't say whether individuals may legally own a
>> brigantine.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>are meaningful.

Lacking 'enabling legistlation', one assumes they're from outside the
US.

>> Did you see Johnny Depp's
>> impersonation of Keith Richards in Pirates of the Caribbean?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>me.  

Kieth will play Johnny's father in the sequel. Art imitates life.

>I gather he's some sort of musician, but I haven't run into mentions of
>him as a superior interpreter of the music that is more likely to interest
>me, and I don't think I've heard any of the conservatory-trained musicians I
>know mention him (positively, negatively or otherwise) offhand ...

The classically-inclined musicians I know call it Art Music, not
Classical Music, and compose their own inspired by the one whose name
I adopted: Richard Wagner, who introduced complexity. I was born a
Quackenbush, changed it because the Marx Brothers despoiled my
surname.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

```
In article <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On Mon, 10 Jan 2005 12:43:09 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
><charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>news:jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com...

[snip]

>>> Things change with time. It's no longer 1789.
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is obsolete. 

I've heard this before, Mr Wagner... that 'the right to keep and bear 
arms' was intended to/should be limited to technology available when the 
Constitution was written... flintlocks, bows and arrows... maybe the 
occaisional smallbore cannon.

This would appear to make as much sense, though, as saying that freedom of 
speech is limited to how far one can halloo into a hailing-trumpet and has 
no application to radio or television... or that freedom of the press is 
limited to the wielding of goose-quills and Gutenberg-machines.

However... the rights of speech and press, in the First Amendment, have 
been shown to apply over changes in technology.  For what reasons would 
the right of the Second Amendment be handled any differently?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-11T03:35:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com> <crupc5$ifd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com> <crv543$63b$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 10 Jan 2005 19:03:15 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>the right of the Second Amendment be handled any differently?

Freedom of speech and press is a principle. The right to bear arms is
tied to a technology: the militia. It says so in the Second Amendment.
Now that the technology is obsolete, there's no reason for the people
to bear small arms.

Let's say the most conspiratorial paranoic fears are true. The Feds
are planning to subjugate the people. Do you realistically think Bubba
with a deer rifle is any match against FEMA and SWAT teams? They'd
swat him like a pesky fly. Look what happened to revolutionaries at
Waco and Ruby Ridge. 

All we're doing with right to bear arms is killing each other in
crimes of passion, petty crimes and suicide. We're not defending
ourselves against a standing army.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-01-11T05:21:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cs09b5$g8b$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com> <crv543$63b$1@panix5.panix.com> <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com>`

```
In article <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 10 Jan 2005 19:03:15 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>tied to a technology: the militia.

Speech, Mr Wagner, is not a principle; it is an activity and it can be 
carried via technical means.  The press, Mr Wagner, is not a principle; it 
is a technology.  A militia is neither a technology nor a principle, it is 
an organisation.

>It says so in the Second Amendment.
>Now that the technology is obsolete, there's no reason for the people
>to bear small arms.

Given that the definitions supplied are wrong the conclusion appears to be 
likewise.

>
>Let's say the most conspiratorial paranoic fears are true.

Let's say that the Constitution of the United States of America carries in 
it provisions for Its modification; until such time as this happens then 
It still appears to apply, both the parts one agrees with and the ones 
that one doesn't.

>The Feds
>are planning to subjugate the people. Do you realistically think Bubba
>with a deer rifle is any match against FEMA and SWAT teams? They'd
>swat him like a pesky fly. Look what happened to revolutionaries at
>Waco and Ruby Ridge. 

I'll deal with that when it happens, Mr Wagner... and I've noticed that 
those pesky Vietnamese, without any helicopters or airplanes, failed 
miserably at preventing the USA from implementing Her will in their 
environs...

... and I've noticed that I am the King of England, as well.

>All we're doing with right to bear arms is killing each other in
>crimes of passion, petty crimes and suicide. We're not defending
>ourselves against a standing army. 

Another overbearing generalisation... all it takes, Mr Wagner, are 
citizens using firearms otherwise to prove this assertion wrong.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-12T04:52:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7s98u015q25d0qvdhv7jvai4d16fga126u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com> <crv543$63b$1@panix5.panix.com> <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com> <cs09b5$g8b$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 11 Jan 2005 05:21:25 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>is a technology.  

Courts have interpreted 'speech' to mean communication, which can be
expressed with hand gestures, burning flags and pictures of naked
people. It is not limited to oral sounds.

>The militia is not a technology, it is an organization.

It is an organization with one purpose: to use arms. Whatever you call
it, the militia wielding arms provided by its members is obsolete.

>>It says so in the Second Amendment.
>>Now that the technology is obsolete, there's no reason for the people
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>likewise.

If your majesty says so.


>>The Feds
>>are planning to subjugate the people. Do you realistically think Bubba
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>environs...

Guerrillas do not use armor nor aircraft because they are difficult to
move without detection, thus would reduce mobility. Guerrilla fighting
is all about mobility.

The government's  initial estimate of enemy strength in Vietnam was
225,000. Remember Gen. Westmoreland suing CBS for saying that was a
lie? When the evidence showed it WAS a lie, he dropped the lawsuit.

The Viet Cong and NVA had 600,000 troops. In guerrilla warfare, a
rule of thumb says it takes three regulars to defeat one irregular. We
had 500,000 troops. Winning would have required 1.3M additional US
ground troops. The total size of the US Army in 1969 was 1.5M.

The size of the US Army is now 500,000. That means a guerrilla force
of 100,000 can cause another Vietnam. Our initial estimate of enemy
insurgents in Iraq was 5,000. After killing a few thousand of them, we
raised it to 20,000. 

"The head of Iraq's intelligence service Gen Muhammad Shahwani now
puts the number of insurgents at 200,000, of which 40,000 are said to
be the hard core and the rest active supporters."
http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/middle_east/4145585.stm

We have 150,000 troops, once again 80% of the enemy's head count.  

In both Vietnam and Iraq, the enemy had/has the home field advantage.
They're repelling an invading army. Telling them we're there to give
them democracy doesn't seem to cut it in the hearts and minds of
citizens. They'd rather do it their way, even though their way
demonstrably failed in the past. It's like dealing with teenagers.

We don't have the manpower to win in Iraq. The longer we drag it out,
the greater the inevitable pull-out will damage American prestige.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 41)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-12T19:18:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c6uau0hanrddhrpjsgdpi6nn7hfkm842v5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com> <cs09b5$g8b$1@panix5.panix.com> <7s98u015q25d0qvdhv7jvai4d16fga126u@4ax.com> <cs2tsb$lro$1@panix5.panix.com> <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com> <cs3nht$lee$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Wed, 12 Jan 2005 17:41:05 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On 12-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Define "war".

War is not an independent phenomenon, but the continuation of politics
by different means.
- Carl von Clausewitz

..economic expansion by political rather than by economic means.
- Carroll Quigley

A war is not won if the defeated enemy has not been turned into a
friend.
- Eric Hoffer

War is a ritual, a deadly ritual, not the result of aggressive
self-assertion, but of self-transcending identification. Without
loyalty to tribe, church, flag or ideal, there would be no wars.
- Arthur Koestler

I used to think that the causes of war were predominantly economic. I
came to think that they were more psychological. I am now coming to
think that they are decisively "personal," arising from the defects
and ambitions of those who have the power to influence the currents of
nations.
B. H. Liddell Hart
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 42)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-12T19:34:52+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 43)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-13T03:23:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<54gbu0t96v2t3qfc4k80rlv7nqdense2jq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com> <cs09b5$g8b$1@panix5.panix.com> <7s98u015q25d0qvdhv7jvai4d16fga126u@4ax.com> <cs2tsb$lro$1@panix5.panix.com> <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com> <cs3nht$lee$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c6uau0hanrddhrpjsgdpi6nn7hfkm842v5@4ax.com> <cs3urp$11vr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 12 Jan 2005 11:47:29 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>it to mean -- neither more nor less."?

"economic expansion by political rather than by economic means" is a
definition. So is the often-quoted "continuation of politics
by different means." My point is that war is more than armed
conflict; it is a process that changes a country's political and
economic systems. Its goal is to turn an enemy into a ally and trading
partner.

The process starts with military victory, building an infrastructure
and operating it temporarily. Those steps are done by our military.
They're the first steps in waging war. Then politicians and civilians
handle transition to a new government, the final steps.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vcaeu0pld7edijnf5h0olhp7nembp2sahl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com> <cs09b5$g8b$1@panix5.panix.com> <7s98u015q25d0qvdhv7jvai4d16fga126u@4ax.com> <cs2tsb$lro$1@panix5.panix.com> <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com> <cs3nht$lee$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c6uau0hanrddhrpjsgdpi6nn7hfkm842v5@4ax.com> <cs3urp$11vr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <54gbu0t96v2t3qfc4k80rlv7nqdense2jq@4ax.com> <cs64uv$bja$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Thu, 13 Jan 2005 15:42:09 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 12-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Was that the goal of the Punic wars?

The Punic Wars were more about fear and jealousy than rational
political/military goals. Like today's unknowledgable public, the
Roman public thought the army's job was to destroy the enemy, punch
out and go home. 

Even so, after erasing Carthage, first thing the Romans did was
establish law and order in the rest of the territory under Roman Law.
150 years later, Augustus built a grand city called Colonia Julia
Carthago (Roman Carthage) on the same site. So they DID (eventually)
turn the Carthagean Empire into an ally. (Julius Caesar would have
done it sooner if he hadn't been assasinated.)
```

```
On 13-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >> "economic expansion by political rather than by economic means" is a
> >> definition. So is the often-quoted "continuation of politics
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> out and go home.

It is not unusual at all to have wars that are more about fear and jealousy than
rational political/military goals.

There are better ways to turn enemies into allies and trading partners than
attacking them with military might.   But there aren't better ways to obliterate
them.

War is about lots of things - many of them not rational at all.
```

- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <hrg6u093qjfqom1fdsei2ls7ke1qvvkrmi@4ax.com> <cs09b5$g8b$1@panix5.panix.com> <7s98u015q25d0qvdhv7jvai4d16fga126u@4ax.com> <cs2tsb$lro$1@panix5.panix.com> <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com> <cs3nht$lee$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c6uau0hanrddhrpjsgdpi6nn7hfkm842v5@4ax.com> <cs3urp$11vr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <54gbu0t96v2t3qfc4k80rlv7nqdense2jq@4ax.com> <cs64uv$bja$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vcaeu0pld7edijnf5h0olhp7nembp2sahl@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:vcaeu0pld7edijnf5h0olhp7nembp2sahl@4ax.com...

> 150 years later, Augustus built a grand city called Colonia Julia
> Carthago (Roman Carthage) on the same site.

Are you sure the English "Roman Carthage" is an accurate literal or even
semantic rendering of the Latin "Colonia Julia Carthago"?

From what I can tell, a direct English rendering would yield something
closer to "Julian Colony [at] Carthage".  "Julia" and "Colonia" both modify
"Carthago".

Back-translation from "Roman Carthage" would, I believe, yield "Carthago
Romana".

From my readings, I deduce that it was Julius Caesar that had first sent
landless Roman citizens there to live, thereby re-inhabiting the site and
establishing the colony; Augustus made that colony an *administrative*
center in 29BC.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
In article <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 12 Jan 2005 05:24:11 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

you interrupted.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the infrastructure.

Ahhhhh, 'when done right'.  This seems a variant of the 'No True Scotsman

http://atheism.about.com/od/logicalfallacies/a/notruescotsman.htm.

>If we go in, kill some people and leave, that's
>not war, that's marauding. We should have learned the lesson in

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 41)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-12T23:46:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v3abu0l6ebhd8afqpq1f0r2rak794hl1gh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7s98u015q25d0qvdhv7jvai4d16fga126u@4ax.com> <cs2tsb$lro$1@panix5.panix.com> <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com> <cs3p1h$keq$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 12 Jan 2005 13:07:45 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <onhau0ln9vfdrd2o0ei5qfj9es74ofld20@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>>> last time I looked, Mr Wagner, war was all about 
>>>one organisation implementing will upon the environs of another, has 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://atheism.about.com/od/logicalfallacies/a/notruescotsman.htm.

According to strategists such as Liddell Hart, the winner imposes its
will by:
1. Defeating the military
2. Constructing infrastructure and civil law, 
     operating it temporarily
3. Installing a democratic government
4. Training the new government on system operation 
5. Engaging the new country in commerce

Japan and Germany are examples. Defeating the military and then
walking away doesn't accomplish anything; it's lose-lose.

>>>>In both Vietnam and Iraq, the enemy had/has the home field advantage.
>>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>you contradict your own assertion.

Guerillas escape detection by hiding. The best way is by mingling with
the civilian population (as in Iraq). Others are by going underground
(as in Vietnam) or into caves (as in Afghanistan). Before satellites,
mountains were fairly good hiding places. Kentuckians would try hiding
in the Cumberlands, where they'd be sitting ducks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-11T09:40:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cs132h$24l0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com> <crupc5$ifd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com> <crv543$63b$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:crv543$63b$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com>,

> However... the rights of speech and press, in the First Amendment, have
> been shown to apply over changes in technology.  For what reasons would
> the right of the Second Amendment be handled any differently?

Thank you, Doc, I agree.  And my further point is that The Sorts Of Arms a
private citizen may be presumed to own -- even *without* the Second
Amendment -- include fully-armed battleships (Article 1.8.11).  They
included that *before and during* the Revolutionary war, during the
Constitutional Convention, and during the War of 1812 as a matter of
*practice*.  The US decided not to *issue* such letters formally for the
first time in 1898, relative to the Spanish-American War, and didn't sign an
international treaty agreeing not to do so until 1907.

I see nothing *constitutional* that *restricts* that; the Second Amendment
*emphasizes* it.   Without that implicit permission Letters of Marque and
Reprisal couldn't reasonably be issued *to US Citizens* by Congress, and I
see no such constitutional restriction.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-11T09:34:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cs12m1$24is$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com> <crmrqe$19pe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com> <crupc5$ifd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com...

> Letters of Marque are now of historic interest only.

Except that they offer proof that the Framers of the Constitution envisioned
the possibility that *private individuals* -- citizens or otherwise -- might
own what in that time would have been considered Weapons of Mass
Destruction -- and that *that was OK*.

>The US stopped
> using them in the 19th century, after signing the Declaration of
> Paris.

BZZZZT!!   WRONG!!  The US was *not* a signator to the 1856 Declaration of
Paris.  The US did not formally agree to refrain from issuing Letters of
Marque and Reprisal as a matter of general foreign policy until it signed
the 1907 Hague Convention.  During the 1898 Spanish-American War, the US
agreed *in that conflict* not to issue such Letters *even though* it was
*not* a signator to the Declaration of Paris.

It's not clear to me that the US Congress *actually issued* Letters of
Marque and Reprisal after the War of 1812 (although privateers were used by
the Texas Navy in the War of Independence in 1836, and were used by the CSA
against the North during the Civil War).   I don't think they did so in the
War with Mexico.

> Their views on principles remain valid. Their writing about technology
> is obsolete.

Where is the line drawn about the use of technology to maintain the
principles they espoused?  Why is it inappropriate to apply the principles
to whatever technology happens to come up with?

> Letters of Marque are clearly obsolete.

I happen to agree with you as a matter of *opinion*, but I cannot agree that
it is either clearly the only valid conclusion or, for that matter, fact.

> So is the notion of State
> Militia to protect us from federal standing armies -- they've never
> been employed.

That they have not been employed is irrelevant.

> So where does that leave the people, with their arms? Given a
> Constitution that's very difficult to amend, it leaves them in limbo
> .. buffeted by court decisions on one side and administrative
> bureaucrats on the other. The Constitution is not a solid island of
> surety that many long for.

What of that *sizeable* part of the American Electorate that ranks it
somewhere above the First Five Books of the Bible in terms of divine
inspiration?


> No, I still don't see it. Article 1.8.11 doesn't grant any rights to
> US citizens. It assumes privateers will be available, primarily from
> other countries or no country, without giving Americans permission to
> be privateers. The only permission it grants, in the Second Amendment,
> is referred to in Article 1.8.12.

The presumption is that privateers exist, and there is *no* presumption that
they will be "primarily from other countries or no country".  I *believe*,
in fact, the overwhelming majority of *privateers* were *American* ships
with *American* captains and *American* crews.  In the War of 1812, there
were 23 ships in the US Navy and 517 "official" privateers.   Maryland alone
had sent out 40 armed privateers before the end of the first year of the
war.  Where's the evidence for the presumption that privateers are primarily
furriners?

> It would not be appropriate to issue a Letter of Marque to a private
> army armed with thirty carriage-mounted guns.

My point is that it would be appropriate for a US citizen to own thirty
large guns whether those guns were considered arms suitable for militia and
mounted on carriages (2nd Amendment) or suitable for use by a privateer and
mounted on board a ship (Article 1.8.11).

> Lacking 'enabling legistlation', one assumes they're from outside the
> US.

One does, does one?   What historical evidence do you have for that
presumption?   The premise that a US citizen is only permitted to do what a
Federal law expressly allows him to do?  That one's new to me ...

> Kieth will play Johnny's father in the sequel. Art imitates life.

And that's supposed to be of interest to me ... exactly why?   Like as not
if I see the sequel it will be as a family afternoon with my foster
daughter.  Somehow I doubt that such a film will have the impact on me that
"Battleship Potemkin", "Max Havelaar", "Dodeskaden" or even "Fargo" have
had.

 > The classically-inclined musicians I know call it Art Music, not
> Classical Music,

The classically-*trained* musicians I know refer to it as "serious" music,
if they're trying to reserve the term "classical" to indicate the period
that stretches from Glueck and Salieri through early Beethoven.  I've only
heard dilettantes refer to it as "art music".

> and compose their own

Well, goody for them.

> inspired by the one whose name
> I adopted: Richard Wagner,

Well, goody for you.   I'm not that impressed by Wagner; I find his work
more than a little self-indulgent (though not quite so much so as
Tchaikowski).  Give me Mahler, or better still, Hindemith!

>  who introduced complexity.

*WHAT*???     Surely you jest!   I can see an argument for Wagner
introducing concepts like "leitmotif" into the realm of opera, and of
eliminating the "numbers opera" and a more complete fusion of musical and
dramatic elements to form "music drama".  But *complexity*?

Have you not looked at the works of Bach (who died 63 years before Wagner
was born), much less those of Orlando diLasso (who died 91 years after Bach
died) or Thomas Tallis (who died nine years before diLasso).  Check out the
score for Tallis' "Spem in Alium" for linear complexity, or that of
diLasso's "Prophetiae Sybillarum" for harmonic adventurism.  Then come back
and convince me that Wagner is fundamentally innovative for *complexity*.
Or for that matter, consider the *architecture* of either the Art of Fugue
or the Mass in B Minor by Bach.

I've actually sung in the chorus of full-scale productions of Dutchman,
Meistersinger and Rienzi, and have listened through the Ring Cycle many
times.   I've tried to listen to Tannhaeuser, Lohengrin, Tristan and
Parsifal on several occasions.  Bo-ring.  I'm not a huge fan of Wagner or of
the Late Romantics in general.    Way too self-indulgent.

> I was born a
> Quackenbush, changed it because the Marx Brothers despoiled my
> surname.

Oh, there's a good reason.  How did your parents like that?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-13T07:24:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2n4cu0h41qioj6evo43lspu9eppjfe584o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<crf3ek$24a8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <gu4nt09hir9otrgl027q10m3rsrij8ghm8@4ax.com> <crh9vg$ig4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6hjot01ra1o0lhl79ba6fap5coh99qv2j5@4ax.com> <crkium$2pvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cggtt0pel8lt3hn2hmp0ei49l5idloqdvq@4ax.com> <crmrqe$19pe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jguut0paokdl3eb0t1bncffn1254pc2eri@4ax.com> <crupc5$ifd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com> <cs12m1$24is$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 11 Jan 2005 09:34:18 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:peu5u01re3fascebdunqchrcdn37ushvsu@4ax.com...


>> No, I still don't see it. Article 1.8.11 doesn't grant any rights to
>> US citizens. It assumes privateers will be available, primarily from
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>Federal law expressly allows him to do?  That one's new to me ...

Let's suppose the Cobol Standard was the US Constitution. In this
parable:
Cobol = The Feds
Operating Systems = States
Programmers = citizens
Daemons = privateers
Device drivers = military
Memory access = guns
IO port access = cannons
LPARs = other countries

The Founding Fathers, coming from assembly language backgrounds, wrote
in Article 1.8.11 'Cobol  shall have the authority to call daemons.'
It doesn't say one way or the other whether daemons can be written in
Cobol, it just assumes they'll always be available. 

Programmers, fearing a despotic Cobol, insist on a Second Amendment
reading 'Unfettered memory access being essential to programming,
neither Cobol nor operating systems shall limit a program's access to
the machine's memory.'

Years later daemons become obsolete. They are replaced by services and
device drivers. 

Then it came to pass that LPARs were invented. Programmers argued the
Second Amendment gave them unrestricted access to memory of other
virtual machines on the same box. Operating system providers were
unmoved; citizens could not own nukes.

Then multi-tasking was invented. At first, Cobol programs had access
to the memory of other tasks because of the Second Amendment.  Bad
things happened. Other tasks got killed because Cobol programs stepped
on them, by accident or deliberately. Cobol programs were killing
themselves. Someone points out we've never needed unrestricted memory
access; we can access variables by name. Other languages, such as C,
once had it and were destroyed by it. Well-mannered programmers get
along fine without it. Let's delete it from the Standard.

Second Amendment advocates reluctantly agree to limiting access to the
task's own memory; machine guns are banned. For good measure, IO port
access is also revoked, since daemons are obsolete; cannons are
banned.  Hardware engineers invent The Safety, which stops writing
over procedural code. That is received as a good thing.

But wait, someone protests, they said Cobol could call daemons. It is
common knowledge that daemons need access to the memory of others AND
IO ports. If Cobol can call daemons, WE can write them. Somebody has
to; they don't write themselves. Never mind that daemons are obsolete,
we should STILL be able to issue IO commands and access anyone's
memory.

Is this a valid argument?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T21:58:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MjECd.189369$O24.32881@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<59pvd.7413$ZP5.7342@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com>`

```
I may or may not LIKE it, but I don't see anything at ALL ambiguous about

"the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, >shall not be infringed."

"the people" is not limited and what the amendment says is that no government 
(or other institution) shall be allowed to get in the way of the "right to keep 
and bear arms".

It is true that the MOTIVATION (allowing for a "well regulated Militia") 
"limits" the purpose of  the amendment but it does not make what the amendment 
says ambiguous or unclear.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T07:17:58+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ "Right to bear arms" (was: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T14:12:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dBSCd.228686$2W1.22704@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <4%FCd.10$fR3.8@fe40.usenetserver.com> <d25nt09btroebmlnp3s9nu1vv76pnu46ej@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:d25nt09btroebmlnp3s9nu1vv76pnu46ej@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 04 Jan 2005 17:52:29 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Although a member of the ACLU, I am *not* a constitutional attorney.  I have NOT 
done a search on that specific NY state law.  HOWEVER,

A) many "gun" laws *are* (have been) chanllenged on "constitutional basis".  (If 
that one hasn't been - and I don't know this - it might be in the future).  Some 
have been found to be constitutionally acceptable while others have not.

B) "In general" all of the "bill of rights" amendments have been dealt with at 
various times and places as "trade offs". Certainly, the most famous (as far as 
I know) was the "decision" that "freedom of speech" does NOT allow one to 
(falsely) yell 'fire" in a crowded theatre.

It is possible (but not something that I know for a fact) that some (federal) 
court has determined that the "right" to bear "sub-machne gun nor a rocket 
propelled grenade" in NY State is overriden by some other constitutional right.

  ***

The fact that the amendment is CLEAR that the government shall not "Infringe" on 
such a right does NOT mean that there are not other constitutional rights that 
may (in certain cases) inter-act with this right.

P.S.
   A quick search of the Bill of Rights (first 10 amendments to the 
constitution) indicates that only one other amendment indicates its 
"motivation".

Amendment IV says,

"The right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and 
effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures, shall not be violated, and 
no warrants shall issue, but upon probable cause, supported by oath or 
affirmation, and particularly describing the place to be searched, and the 
persons or things to be seized. "

I don't think either the 4th *or* 2nd Amendments provide "motivation" as a 
"restriction" on the amendment.  Both simply provide the (then current thinking) 
that was behind the amendment.

If there were an amendment that said,

"In order to provide adequate roads for stage-coaches, the federal government 
shall provide for adequately paved roads"

this would NOT be invalidated by the "end" of stage-coaches.

Any attempt to "limit" the CURRENT impact of the 2nd amendment based on a 
(perceived) current lack of necessity for "well armed militia" (much less that 
the right to bear arms doesn't seem to - today - provide for a well-armed 
militia) is simply an irrelevant argument.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Right to bear arms" (was: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T12:07:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41DC2CFB.228C27BD@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <4%FCd.10$fR3.8@fe40.usenetserver.com> <d25nt09btroebmlnp3s9nu1vv76pnu46ej@4ax.com> <dBSCd.228686$2W1.22704@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

As you probably know, when "motor cars/horseless carriages" were
invented there were passed in some places laws that stated that each
such car had to be preceded  by a man on foot carrying a red flag so as
to warn the people and the horses they were controlling to get out of
the way.

Following your logic - as I understand it - then these laws, if still on
the books, should still apply and every car-driver could be nailed by
them!

One might also argue that the right to bear arms doesn't carry with it
the right to USE arms.  

Part of this post states that the militia is to protect the people
against the government.  Hogwash!  And even if it were true, do you
think that a ragtag bunch of people armed with one or two guns each
could protect themselves from the Government in the form of its Army?  I
think not. 

PL
```

```
On  5-Jan-2005, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> > Any attempt to "limit" the CURRENT impact of the 2nd amendment based on a
> > (perceived) current lack of necessity for "well armed militia" (much less
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> them!

Your statement of "some places" is a plural.    Could you name two such places
and tell us whether they are still on the books?

=============================

If they are still on the books, they should be enforced.   But if they are still
on the books, they should be repealed.    If we want our drivers to be bound by
law, we should do one or the other.

The 2nd amendment is on the books.    It should be enforced or repealed.   If we
want our politicians to be bound by The Constitution, we should do one or the
other.
```

- **References:** `<V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <4%FCd.10$fR3.8@fe40.usenetserver.com> <d25nt09btroebmlnp3s9nu1vv76pnu46ej@4ax.com> <dBSCd.228686$2W1.22704@news.easynews.com> <41DC2CFB.228C27BD@mb.sympatico.ca> <crhcs9$7gq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On  5-Jan-2005, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> =============================
In England in 1865, the "Locomotive on Highways Act" was passed
(popularly referred to as the Red Flag Act).  It also limited road
vehicles to 4mph.  Wasn't rescinded until 1896.  There was also
something similar in Vienna.

There weren't laws quite as draconian passed in the U.S., but see the
following for examples of restrictions:

//www.sandiago.edu/lrc/vehicle.html#local_traffic

//equisearch.com/humor/itsthelaw07291a/


or just do a search for "horseless carriage laws".

Some of the laws mentioned on these two sites are stated to be on the
books still.

> 
> If they are still on the books, they should be enforced.   But if they are still
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> other.

My point was that no law is cast in stone; and that if circumstances
change the laws ought to be changed to reflect them (although of course
I know that realistically there isn't enough time to do this even if
everyone on earth was employed at it).  As far as the 2nd amendment is
concerned - a very good case could be made that there is no longer a
need for a militia AS IT WAS THOUGHT OF then.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Right to bear arms" (was: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T22:56:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zg_Cd.244543$lR6.42352@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <33q5j6F433d4eU1@individual.net> <f6ugt05d7g4pvktujv0sueomn1h29kcv7e@4ax.com> <crecs0$83m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qd0mt0tempjpc0ujpss46hpcvijn4msn1q@4ax.com> <4%FCd.10$fR3.8@fe40.usenetserver.com> <d25nt09btroebmlnp3s9nu1vv76pnu46ej@4ax.com> <dBSCd.228686$2W1.22704@news.easynews.com> <41DC2CFB.228C27BD@mb.sympatico.ca> <crhcs9$7gq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <41DC6844.5EA2946B@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
But my point was that REGARDLESS of whether or not there is a current need for a 
"militia" *OR* whether or not guaranteeing the "right to bear arms" actually 
"insures" the existence of a "well armed militia", that the 2nd Amendment makes 
a statement about what the government may or may not do - and that the reference 
to the militia is an explanation of the (original) motivation and has NOTHING to 
do with what "right" the 2nd Amendment does (or does not) provide.

P.S.  Personally, I am in the group that would like to see a new amendment 
"repealing" the 2nd Amendment - but I am also among those who fear "tampering 
with" the Constitution (including its amendments) for anything but the MOST 
important reasons.
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> But my point was that REGARDLESS of whether or not there is a current need for a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> do with what "right" the 2nd Amendment does (or does not) provide.

I can appreciate what you say, but I feel that since the framers of the
amendment felt it necessary to provide a rationale that they didn't see
it as applying at all times and under all circumstances.  It's a sort of
sunset clause with unspecified duration.  You're quite right that as
long as the 2nd amendment remains in the constitution then it
(supposedly) constrains the government.
> 
> P.S.  Personally, I am in the group that would like to see a new amendment
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
Agreed on both points.

Did you ever read Robert Heinlein's "The moon is a harsh mistress"?  At
one point his hero proposes a branch of government whose sole purpose is
to cancel laws!  Might be something in the idea!

PL
```

```
On Thu, 6 Jan 2005 14:35:06 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>And I agree with that point.  And I reiterate:
>The 2nd amendment is on the books.    It should be enforced or repealed.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'd be quite satisfied it it were repealed.   But don't wiggle around it.

Three-fourths of the States will never vote for repeal.

When lawyers smell blood in the water, they'll be all over the gun
industry like sharks on a sinking whale. In the news:

"Washington, DC (10/20/04) - Recently, the Indiana Supreme Court, Ohio
Supreme Court, and appellate courts in New Jersey and Illinois have
reinstated cases brought by cities in those states. In the last few
months, courts have also approved a record $4.4 million of settlements
against gun sellers and manufacturers in cases brought by gun violence
victims. This includes an unprecedented $2.5 million settlement in a
suit brought by victims of the DC-area sniper attacks against gun shop
Bull's Eye Shooter Supply of Tacoma, Washington, and gun maker
Bushmaster Firearms of Windham, Maine. Those victims also brought suit
alleging that the gun shop and gun maker acted negligently and created
a public nuisance. Gun shop Bull's Eye claimed that it "lost" 238 guns
in a three year period, including the DC sniper's Bushmaster assault
weapon.

Pennsylvania (10/20/04) - The ruling in Allegheny County Sportsmen's
League v. Rendell holds that Pennsylvania law allows police to keep a
database of handgun sales records. The gun lobby had argued that such
records must be destroyed, citing a state law prohibiting the
maintenance of gun "ownership" records. The Court rejected this
challenge, declaring that "maintenance of a database of handgun sales
is proper" under Pennsylvania law, as such a database tracks only
handgun sales records, not ownership. The court relied in part on a
friend of the court amicus brief filed by law enforcement and gun
violence prevention groups, including the Brady Center to Prevent Gun
Violence, the Pennsylvania Million Mom March and the Pennsylvania
Chiefs of Police Association.

Philadelphia, PA (8/20/04) - A Pennsylvania gun dealer has settled a
pioneering lawsuit brought by a woman whose seven-year-old son was
unintentionally killed near his Philadelphia home with a gun sold by
the dealer. The settlement will have nationwide implications for gun
dealers who supply traffickers with guns - transactions which occur
everyday in gun shops around the country.

The suit, brought by Tennille Jefferson against Williamsport gun
dealer Sauers Trading, charged Sauers with supplying ten guns to gun
trafficker Perry Bruce. Although Sauers sold the guns to Bruce
legally, Bruce purchased the handguns repeatedly, with thousands of
dollars in cash, even while telling Sauers he was unemployed and on
welfare. The guns were trafficked to Philadelphia, where one was found
under a parked car by a group of children and used to unintentionally
shoot and kill Nafis Jefferson on April 19, 1999. "

http://www.gunlawsuits.org/
```

```
> >I'd be quite satisfied it it were repealed.   But don't wiggle around it.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> industry like sharks on a sinking whale. In the news:

I was upset when the gun salesman was destroyed with the Columbine High School
incident.   And I was upset at the way lawyers wiggled into getting tobacco
moneys (because one manufacturer had a cartoon camel).    I don't shoot and I
don't smoke, but strongly believe that our practice of getting around laws
leaves us very vulnerable to those who don't like things I do care about.
```

```
Perhaps we should start recalling politicians when they don't begin
impeachment procedings against activist judges? Because the Congress of
the USofA has not impeached judges that see things in the Constitution
that aren't there (and the reverse), we now have a very sorry state of
affairs.

And did anyone notice what the 10th Amendment contains? Anything not
specifically defined to the state is reserved to the people (or words
to that effect)?

Perhaps the citizens of the country should be deported unless they are
going to do their constitutionally mandated responsibilities?
Later,
Steve.T
```

```
"steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:1105628697.542834.51650@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
> Perhaps we should start recalling politicians when they don't begin
> impeachment procedings against activist judges? Because the Congress of
> the USofA has not impeached judges that see things in the Constitution
> that aren't there (and the reverse), we now have a very sorry state of
> affairs.

I think you'd also have to nullify Marbury v. Madison (handed down only
fourteen years after the ratification of the constitution!), and the only
way I can think of to do that is by amendment; after over two hundred years
of precedent I seriously doubt that the Supreme Court is going to reverse
the principle of judicial review!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
steve.t wrote:
> Perhaps we should start recalling politicians when they don't begin
> impeachment procedings against activist judges? Because the Congress of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> to that effect)?

I believe the phrase is "left to the several states" - meaning, in their 
view, the decision should be made in Nashville, Albany, Sacramento, 
Montgomery, etc. instead of Washington D.C.

> Perhaps the citizens of the country should be deported unless they are
> going to do their constitutionally mandated responsibilities?

The "right" to vote is not necessarily a "responsibility" to vote.  If 
one hasn't concerned themselves with the issues, I'd just as soon they 
stay the heck away from the voting booth.  :)
```

```
I wouldn't 'hurt anyone' -- even when guarding ammo or tactical nukes.  
You can always count on the stupidity  of government to help out in a 
pinch...
```

```
On 10-Jan-2005, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> I will search for some numbers.  I know that the 1st Brigade of USAREC
> (northeast) is traditionally the lowest producer of enlistments, but
> I'll secure some concrete numbers.

Thanks

> It might be worth noting that California was a solid red state until
> just a few elections ago.  It still is everywhere but LA and SF.

There are a few maps of red and blue counties available on the Web.   It's
interesting to see that across the country, the split was urban vs rural.   Most
of the California coast is blue - but most of the California coast is urban.  
(I remember when Pat Brown was the first Democrat to be re-elected governor of
California).

I'm of the opinion that the election was more image than substance.   Rural
people associate fast talking city folk with used car salesmen and con men.   
Urban people associate slow talking country folk with stupid hicks.    City folk
think if you don't interrupt, you aren't interested.   These are images, not
public policy.
```

```
On Mon, 10 Jan 2005 15:47:03 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On 10-Jan-2005, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>public policy.

I remember George W. Bush's first run for office, to represent West
Texas in the House. He was knowledgable then. He was perceived  to be
a fast-talking carpet-bagger from New England. His opponent, Kent
Hance (whom I know), played on the perception with statements like
"When George W. Bush was attending Yale and Harvard, I was attending
Texas Tech." Kent projected a 'country' image and won by a wide
margin. George W. didn't forget the lesson he learned.

Now people think Bush is stupid .. and underestimate him. It's an
image he cultivates. When he entered Yale, his SAT score was above
1200. That's not the score of a dummy. His graduation marks were in
the middle of the class, but remember this was Yale, where the
unpublished SAT of entering freshmen is probably 1450 (vs. 1100 at an
average university).
```

```
On Thu, 13 Jan 2005 07:37:27 GMT, jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

>On 10 Jan 2005, you wrote in comp.lang.cobol:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>people with 1250+).

Good point. Yale's average *today*  is 1450. In 1964, when Bush
entered, it was probably 1250, surely no higher than 1300. In the good
old days, 1000 was very good, 1250 was near genius. Rice used to brag
about 1250 and people were impressed. 

-- quote -- 
# It's now possible to miss five questions and get a "perfect" score
of 1600.

# Before recentering, 25 students at most made perfect scores, but now
as many as 2,500 could.

http://www.ncpa.org/pi/edu/sept97b.html

Bush's report card. Add 566 and 640 to get SAT (then CEEB).
http://www.iuptown.com/YaleProtest/bushs_yale_transcript.htm
```

```
In article <crjiis$ju4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On  5-Jan-2005, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> serving overseas).

Here are the numbers you asked for.  It took some doing, I actually used 
the M$ Excel product -- I really want to exercise some 2nd amendment 
rights upon whomever designed that stupid little Mac128 agent.  (the 
paperclip in the windoze version)

I took the DOD population representation survey and mixed it with the 
2004 electoral vote.  It shows military enlistment by percent of 
population by state and enlistments per electoral vote.  

You can find the full table at "www.zitzelberger.net/index.html".  

The data used was for 2002 enlistments, the most recent year available. 
D.C. is included as a state because I had data for it.  The population 
figures are from the reapportionment census.

Some interesting details:

   - Washington, The first blue state to appear is 19th ranked in 
military enlistment.

   - Only 3 blue states appear in the top 30.

   - The bottom of the table is solid blue, the top, solid red.  This 
tends to support my contention that the army is populated by 'red state 
types'.

Enjoy.
```

```
On 11-Jan-2005, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> Here are the numbers you asked for.  It took some doing, I actually used
> the M$ Excel product -- I really want to exercise some 2nd amendment
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> You can find the full table at "www.zitzelberger.net/index.html".

Very interesting.

Have you looked at a map showing red and blue counties?  (some can be googled).
  The election was very much an urban vs rural thing.

I heard an article on the radio discussing the fact that rural soldiers are much
more likely to be casualties than urban soldiers.    The hypothesis put forth is
that rural soldiers are less likely to see a good job when they return home, so
they are more interested in being the best soldiers that they can be - this is
their career.    Urban soldiers are more likely to be thinking on how to survive
long enough to come home and use the GI bill to go back to school.
```
