```
>the size of a live cartridge (less the bullet).  What nonsense, chamber
>sizes are fixed for a particular gun.

Shell casings are not filled with powder. There's plenty of room for
additional powder.

>Having fired .303 blanks and .303 live the sound is about the same
>level and the powder load was insignificantly different (though it
>would be much faster burning).

I had to look up .303, learned it's WWI era Lee-Enfield similar to our
.30-06. I think the powder load in blanks was higher, even though none
were gas-operated semi-autos. 

>In fact starter pistol rounds are smaller than .22 short rounds.

Again, there's plenty of space for additional powder.

>> So about 1/3 of the bang comes from exploding powder and 2/3 from the
>projectile.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>general figure can be given any credence at all.

Yes, I extrapolated from three times the powder.

>In fact if it were true in any way then 'silencers' would hardly work
>at all.  If 2/3 were from the projectile then that 2/3 would not be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>reduces the peak of the pressure wave.

You talk like an engineer (a good thing).  Silencers don't work well
on supersonic bullets for the reasons you gave. They can deaden the
explosion but can't do much about the zing of of supersonic flight.

>> The sound from the gun is directed; the projectile's sonic boom is
>> omnidirectional.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>make a silly claim that it was 'omnidirectional'.

Ok, it's directed to the front, but not toward the ground.

>The major part of the noise is made by the expanding gasses at the
>point when the bullet leaves the barrel and the residual pressure can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>open.

Muzzle velocity is directly proportional to barrel length.  That's how
we estimated the range of enemy artillery. 

>> Also, the boom-cone is about ten feet above your ears, so its
>footprint is smaller.
>
>Just make it up as you go along, you have no idea how far it was from
>my ears.

Rifle range butts are pretty much the same. 

>In any case, by your theory standing 10 foot from a gun, alongside it,
>should not be much louder than if one was, say, a couple hundred metres
>down range 10 foot from the path.
>
>Based on my experience, this is complete crap.

Have you ever heard a silenced rifle? It makes a dull thud at close
range, a louder zing at the target.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T10:53:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104864785.468067.218700@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7ikkt0hrjisj6q00g779tei067svvb2149@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <V%KAd.3681$Kw1.1871@fe40.usenetserver.com> <vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <1104778898.477277.310300@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <q1mjt0h91jltnub43hkk5ntqnuas8uj3g4@4ax.com> <1104807021.788374.183580@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7ikkt0hrjisj6q00g779tei067svvb2149@4ax.com>`

```
> Shell casings are not filled with powder. There's plenty of room for
> additional powder.

Geez, Robert, when cornered, you just increase the rate that you make
nonsense up.

Cartridges do not rattle when shaken.  They are not '1/3 full'. They
make cartridges of exactly the size required to hold the powder and the
bullet tightly. They do not 'leave space so that they can add more
powder for blanks' (or any other reason).

Shotgun cartridges are quite different from this because they also have
to hold the shot and the wadding or sabot.  But rifle and handgun
cartridges are always full.

>> In fact starter pistol rounds are smaller than .22 short rounds.

> Again, there's plenty of space for additional powder.

The whole point of being a .22 'short' is that it has less powder than
a .22 'long'.  The cartridge size is reduced so that there _isn't_
'plenty of space'.  There is never empty space left in a cartridge.

>> If you knew anything about 'sonic booms' you would not
>> make a silly claim that it was 'omnidirectional'.

> Ok, it's directed to the front, but not toward the ground.

You seem to know even less about sonic booms than even I would credit
you with.

Have you never been under a supersonic aircraft at 500 feet ? (I have).
They are completely and utterly silent 'to the front', they are silent
even after they have passed overhead until they are at about 45 degrees
beyond.  After the bang the sound recedes quickly.

The amount of noise in the bang depends on many different factors, such
as overpressure generated by the lift from the wings (which is thus
proprtional to weight), interference from the many parts of the
structure, and is also an accumulation of the noise from the engine.
That is, all the time the plane was completely silent as it approched
the noise it was making (engine etc) just hadn't reached you yet.  It
reaches you all at once.

None of this applies to bullets (but does to rockets) because they
don't, of themselves, make noise.  They have no wings to create
overpressure from lift, they are only a single shape so have no
interference.  Bullets do create a 'sonic boom' but it is trivial
compared to the unsilenced explosion that drives it.

> Silencers .. They can deaden the
> explosion but can't do much about the zing of of supersonic flight.

Exactly.  They deaden much of the explosion.  The 'zing' is just a
small part of the noise.
Your 'few people know' is just more misinformation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T19:22:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<creqf6$g2t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1104864785.468067.218700@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```

On  4-Jan-2005, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> None of this applies to bullets (but does to rockets) because they
> don't, of themselves, make noise.  They have no wings to create
> overpressure from lift, they are only a single shape so have no
> interference.  Bullets do create a 'sonic boom' but it is trivial
> compared to the unsilenced explosion that drives it.

The bullets do make noise.   The sonic boom we hear is similar to the crack of a
whip (for the same reason).   It is not similar to the boom of the firing.

Shotguns are loud, even though they do not create shots that break the sound
barrier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T13:36:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104874616.767756.4040@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<creqf6$g2t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cpn2m3$ak4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1104864785.468067.218700@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <creqf6$g2t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
>>  they don't, of themselves, make noise.

> The bullets do make noise.

They don't, of themselves, make noise.  Unlike a Jet Aircraft, bullets
do not generate noise while stationary (and running).

>> Bullets do create a 'sonic boom'

> The sonic boom we hear is similar to the crack of a whip (for the
same reason).

Mostly.  A supersonic plane's sonic boom is not just the 'bow wave', it
is also the accumulation of noise from its engines, etc.  When a
subsonic jet flies towards you you can hear it for all the time it
approaches, the sound energy is spread over a long time.  When flying
at Mach 1 towards you all that sound energy arrives at the same time,
along with the 'bow wave' of pressure.

When it flies faster, such as a Concorde at Mach 2.2, it gets
'downrange' faster than the sound can get to ground level, so it gets
60 deg from vertically above (ie is only 30 deg above the horizon)
before you can hear _anything_.  Then there is a diffuse boom (from ten
miles above) followed by a rumble for some minutes from points along
its track as the noise arrives, initially from directly above, then
moving along the track both ways.

Those 4 Olympus in after burning are _loud_.  I haven't been under a
Concorde at full speed but have been at the end of a runway as one took
off (with afterburners) over me.

Whether the 'crack of the whip' is _entirely_ due to the tip going
sonic is another argument.  For example I get exactly the same noise
from a strap by folding it over and, holding it in two hands,
'snapping' it.  The strap never gets close to moving at supersonic
speed, but the air ejected laterally probably does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T01:47:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a39mt09eq7aee37umviu1ibjbil7h04o3q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <1104778898.477277.310300@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <q1mjt0h91jltnub43hkk5ntqnuas8uj3g4@4ax.com> <1104807021.788374.183580@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7ikkt0hrjisj6q00g779tei067svvb2149@4ax.com> <1104864785.468067.218700@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 4 Jan 2005 10:53:05 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Shell casings are not filled with powder. There's plenty of room for
>> additional powder.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>powder for blanks' (or any other reason).

Go to this Web site:
http://www.loadyourown.com/loaddata/sdata.asp

Select Rifle - .45 Colt and look at the range of loads. They go from 7
to 30 grains. Then try Pistol - .357 Magnum, which ranges from 3 to
20. Unexpectedly, muzzle velocity is not linear to the load.

>Shotgun cartridges are quite different from this because they also have
>to hold the shot and the wadding or sabot.  But rifle and handgun
>cartridges are always full.

No, they're not.

>>> In fact starter pistol rounds are smaller than .22 short rounds.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>'plenty of space'.  There is never empty space left in a cartridge.

I can't find data on .22 blank loads, so the question is undecided.
It's safe to say explosion rate is faster.

>>> If you knew anything about 'sonic booms' you would not
>>> make a silly claim that it was 'omnidirectional'.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>beyond.  After the bang the sound recedes quickly.

If the aircraft is traveling faster than sound, by definition the
sound will arrive after it passes. Trailing behind is an
omnidirectional cone of noise. The higher the aircraft or bullet, the
smaller the intersection of cone and ground.

>The amount of noise in the bang depends on many different factors, such
>as overpressure generated by the lift from the wings (which is thus
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>reaches you all at once.

Yes, if it's traveling close to the speed of sound, sound is
accumulating. If it's pulling out of a dive, accumulated sound arrives
all at once. 

But objects that issue no sound (such as a bullet) can still produce
sonic booms. The act of traveling fast produces a pressure wave.

>None of this applies to bullets (but does to rockets) because they
>don't, of themselves, make noise.  They have no wings to create
>overpressure from lift, they are only a single shape so have no
>interference.  Bullets do create a 'sonic boom' but it is trivial
>compared to the unsilenced explosion that drives it.

Everyone who's been around guns knows that rifles (and artillery)
produce a 'crack' that's distinct from the explosion sound.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Science versus religion: Is compromise impossible?

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T18:49:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104893342.934270.38830@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<a39mt09eq7aee37umviu1ibjbil7h04o3q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vtn7t0h3bnqaaljti633a72o6fq93rr36t@4ax.com> <zR_Ad.7505$Kw1.2025@fe40.usenetserver.com> <7dk9t0diouj568spjbchdl61v28iqim3a8@4ax.com> <33n6eeF4229heU1@individual.net> <jkjdt0lv5msqckvtn2bjoamujjb4n9n2qm@4ax.com> <1104778898.477277.310300@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <q1mjt0h91jltnub43hkk5ntqnuas8uj3g4@4ax.com> <1104807021.788374.183580@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7ikkt0hrjisj6q00g779tei067svvb2149@4ax.com> <1104864785.468067.218700@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <a39mt09eq7aee37umviu1ibjbil7h04o3q@4ax.com>`

```
> They go from 7 to 30 grains.

You will also note that they use different bullets and these will be
set with different depths.  The powder is not to be left loose, the
bullet should seat on top of it.

> Unexpectedly, muzzle velocity is not linear to the load.

Well I certainly wouldn't expect it to be linear with load.  For a
start it wouldn't be linear because the force required increases as the
square of the velocity (simple physics).  Secondly different powder
types are used and these would have different expansion
characteristics.

> Trailing behind is an omnidirectional cone of noise.

You should learn what 'omni' means.

The sound does spread laterally.

> The higher the aircraft or bullet, the smaller the intersection of
cone and ground.

Where did you get that from ?  Perhaps you could explain in what way it
would be 'smaller'.  For example a Concorde leaves a sonic boom path a
few miles wide while that of a Tornado 'on the deck'  would be a few
hundred yards.

>>  Bullets do create a 'sonic boom'

> But objects that issue no sound (such as a bullet) can still produce
> sonic booms. The act of traveling fast produces a pressure wave.

Exactly. As I said.  They can also produce noise when subsonic, but
much less.

> Everyone who's been around guns knows that rifles (and artillery)
> produce a 'crack' that's distinct from the explosion sound.

Yes, but your original was:

RW>> Contrary to popular belief, a gun's bang does not come
RW>> from exploding powder; it's a sonic boom caused by the projectile.

Now, at last, you agree:

1)  there is "an explosion sound" which _is_ "a gun's bang".
2)  the 'crack' is only a part of the sound - a small part in many
cases.
3)  "Popular belief" is actually right, the "bang" does come from
exploding powder.
```

## Thread: Pro*COBOL and CLOB Columns — 6 this month / 16 total

_Thread started [2004-12](2004-12.md#t-51484d114c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and CLOB Columns

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-01T08:50:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RvtBd.54156$uM5.32831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cr1g71$m5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1103294765.387986.37710@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <GULAd.3808$Kw1.582@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cr1666$eme$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <edb8t0tm02tssna275p6db65vjep9j3q3s@4ax.com> <cr1g71$m5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 30-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> (snip)

I think this is the way your shop has Endevor configured.  All our 
source and object code is managed by Endevor and I am able to use 
PROCESS and CBL directives in my source code.  I can store compile 
options in the source program.

Our Endevor processors for COBOL specify the version of COBOL to use 
(although we are removing Endevor processors for OS/VS COBOL and VS 
COBOL II, since they are no longer supported), then whether the 
program is batch or CICS, then whether the program uses DB2, then some 
specialized codes for copybook search paths.

We used to have specialized COBOL processors for products that 
required statically linked Vendor code, such as H&W SYSB-II, 
CA-Interlink TCP/IP support, and IBM Crypto, but the Endevor 
administrators seem to have solved that problem.

So we have a very specialized naming convention for our Endevor COBOL 
processors:

CO3B     Compile Enterprise COBOL, batch
CO3C     Compile Enterprise COBOL, CICS
CO3BD    Compile Enterprise COBOL, batch with DB2
CO3CD    Compile Enterprise COBOL, CICS with DB2
CO3CNNNP Compile Enterprise COBOL, CICS, with only production level 
copybooks
CO2B     Compile COBOL II, batch (obsolete now)

My biggest gripe with Endevor is that now we use parallel development, 
so that a single program may be in several different stages of 
development for multiple releases.  It's a nightmare to intervene for 
a production fix, jump over all the in-flight versions to install into 
PROD, and then retrofit the fixes to the release level projects.  It's 
complicated enough that sometimes we avoid applying production fixes 
because the source code is locked down for a release implementation 
that is several weeks away.

But we also manage the compile listings with Endevor and SAR, and we 
no longer retain hardcopies because we can reprint them anytime.

Our Endevor is also configured with several different environments, 
and we only compile into the earliest environment and stage, and then 
migrate the executable to each following stage.  It is possible to 
configure Endevor so that the program will be automatically recompiled 
  as it is migrated to each successive stage, development, 
certification, PROD.  Of course, the Endevor MOVE processors must 
correctly handle DB2 binds for each environment, but that setup seems 
to work fairly well.

YMMV, IANAL, and all sorts of other disclaimers may apply.

With kindest regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and CLOB Columns

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-01T16:39:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r0kdt0djfpt87dst0u2an7jbkgben511kg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1103294765.387986.37710@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <GULAd.3808$Kw1.582@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cr1666$eme$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <edb8t0tm02tssna275p6db65vjep9j3q3s@4ax.com> <cr1g71$m5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RvtBd.54156$uM5.32831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Sat, 01 Jan 2005 08:50:57 GMT, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>

>Our Endevor is also configured with several different environments, 
>and we only compile into the earliest environment and stage, and then 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>certification, PROD.  

I was assigned to write a one-time program that absolutely HAD to run
that night. I compiled it in Test (with no special options) and tested
it there. I even snuck into production, copied the executable and ran
it against production data to make sure it would work.

That night they promoted it to Prod with Endevor and recompiled it
with different options. When executed, it abended. I was the bad guy.

It escapes me why shops recompile tested code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and CLOB Columns

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-01T20:21:31+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sdqdt0h9oa7svlvtohdu2s60en6fvofeuv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1103294765.387986.37710@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <GULAd.3808$Kw1.582@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cr1666$eme$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <edb8t0tm02tssna275p6db65vjep9j3q3s@4ax.com> <cr1g71$m5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RvtBd.54156$uM5.32831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <r0kdt0djfpt87dst0u2an7jbkgben511kg@4ax.com>`

```
On Sat, 01 Jan 2005 16:39:03 GMT Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
wrote:

:>On Sat, 01 Jan 2005 08:50:57 GMT, Arnold Trembley
:><arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

:>>Our Endevor is also configured with several different environments, 
:>>and we only compile into the earliest environment and stage, and then 
:>>migrate the executable to each following stage.  It is possible to 
:>>configure Endevor so that the program will be automatically recompiled 
:>>  as it is migrated to each successive stage, development, 
:>>certification, PROD.  

:>I was assigned to write a one-time program that absolutely HAD to run
:>that night. I compiled it in Test (with no special options) and tested
:>it there. I even snuck into production, copied the executable and ran
:>it against production data to make sure it would work.

:>That night they promoted it to Prod with Endevor and recompiled it
:>with different options. When executed, it abended. I was the bad guy.

:>It escapes me why shops recompile tested code.

To verify that all parts of the module were promoted.

To verify all source pieces exist.

Also, you should be aware that test mode might mask some errors, since more
storage is used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and CLOB Columns

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T15:39:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<credee$8sc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1103294765.387986.37710@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <GULAd.3808$Kw1.582@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cr1666$eme$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <edb8t0tm02tssna275p6db65vjep9j3q3s@4ax.com> <cr1g71$m5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RvtBd.54156$uM5.32831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <r0kdt0djfpt87dst0u2an7jbkgben511kg@4ax.com>`

```

On  1-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >Our Endevor is also configured with several different environments,
> >and we only compile into the earliest environment and stage, and then
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> It escapes me why shops recompile tested code.

Theory is: They want it to work even when some maintenance programmer has to
make some little change in the middle of the night.    So it's an excellent idea
to compile into STG1TEST using Endevor (I hate brand names that misspell words),
guaranteeing that your last test uses the same options as some future
maintenance programmer will have.
```

```
Chris,

Are you running at the latest patch level from Oracle? You can search

SimonT.
```

#### ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and CLOB Columns

- **From:** "Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-06T10:22:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1105035741.126466.37820@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1103294765.387986.37710@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1103294765.387986.37710@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
All:

Many thanks for the replies to this post. I too was under the
impression that a PIC S9(4) COMP would be able to hold a value larger
than 9999, but that is not the case (at least not on our system). If I
try to move anything larger than 9999 to the -LEN variable, it
truncates to the last four digits of the number being moved (i.e. 10000
becomes 0, 10001 becomes 1 ).

The solution Mr. Wagner proposed has given me the ability to resolve my
issue. I did have to make two minor changes though. This variable, can
be used to insert values into a CLOB column successfully. Of course, as
the size of the field becomes larger and larger, it may be more prudent
to use Mr. Wagner's alternative SQL-CLOB variable type and its
associated commands, but for now - this works nicely.

01  LogBuffer.
02  LogBuffer-LEN     PIC S9(9) COMP.
02  LogBuffer-ARR     PIC  X(262146).
EXEC SQL VAR LogBuffer is LONG VARCHAR (242146) END-EXEC.
Again - thanks to all.

Chris
```

```
> Someone mentioned monster seven-trailer trucks traveling the
> Australian outback at 70 mph.

They did ?  Strange that Google can't find that.

> No so.They're called Road Trains and most have two or three trailers.

What is it that is 'No so' ?  Road Trains _are_ 'monster trucks with up
to 7 trailers' and do travel at up to 70 mph.

They have 1, 2, 3, 4 or as many will fit with 53.5 metres.

http://territorypictures.tripod.com/pages/rtrains/rtrainsindex.html

> We have the same in northern Indiana along I-80.

I know how difficult it is for you to accept not being the
best/biggest/most loud mouthed, but those triples in Indiana are not
"the same", or even close.

The limits for Indiana are doubles to 65 feet and 80,000 lb.  Triples
may exist on toll roads  and may be 50% more than those limits, up to
98 feet.

http://www.pilotcars.com/indiana.htm

Road Trains are up to 160 feet (53.5 metres) and 300 tonnes (330,000
lbs). ie twice the size.

> The really big Australian trucks, with 6-7 trailers, travel on
private roads no faster than 40 mph.

Certainly speed is determined by motive power, weight, and air
resistence.  The larger the slower.  But where did you get the nonsense
about 'private roads' ?

http://territorypictures.tripod.com/pages/rtrains/images/bhrtrain.jpg

http://members.ozemail.com.au/~djf01/riding_with_roadtrains.html

"""4. In the Tanami Desert in NT, where there is now a 400 tonne seven
(yes seven) trailer two engine road train servicing "the Granites" gold
mining project. I can't wait to for that one to pass me."""
```

- **References:** `<sqtcr0h86rgg38nee8rufhn3fevql240o7@4ax.com> <1102485864.198828.257240@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <qcddr092lr24vvjhsei9epgf38mdqc0a69@4ax.com> <cpkak4$jqd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3prr0p482odrbaqtb2d8fenanu92ueo94@4ax.com> <cpkv39$2nh$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <t06sr0lgorg5vhq74jrnl45pimvor4a2d2@4ax.com> <33gd0eF3uqbhcU1@uni-berlin.de> <5ra6t0lu4nmd4091mt6fshiuf5qk0mfha4@4ax.com> <1104617170.404234.147860@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 1 Jan 2005 14:06:10 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Someone mentioned monster seven-trailer trucks traveling the
>> Australian outback at 70 mph.
>
>They did ?  Strange that Google can't find that.

I must have read it elsewhere.

>> No so.They're called Road Trains and most have two or three trailers.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>They have 1, 2, 3, 4 or as many will fit with 53.5 metres.

Most tourist pictures show three trailers.

>> We have the same in northern Indiana along I-80.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.pilotcars.com/indiana.htm

That site is misinformed. The standard US triple uses 28 foot trailers
and has a length of 105 feet. A 98 foot triple cannot be made from
readily available equipment. The triples running on I-80 (a toll road)
do not have escort cars, as the site claims they must. 

A standard double, made from 48 foot trailers, is actually 5 feet
longer than a triple. Both carry 120,000 pounds. 

>Road Trains are up to 160 feet (53.5 metres) and 300 tonnes (330,000
>lbs). ie twice the size.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>about 'private roads' ?

From a Web site I can't find again. It showed six-trailer road trains
claimed to be the longest registered for everyday use, used to haul
ore from a mine to a port. I got the impression the mining company had
paid for the road. A picture caption said, "40 is about it". I assumed
that had been translated to miles per hour.

If the limit is 160 feet, that means Aussies are using 20 foot
trailers. 

The big difference is they're running on two-lane roads. Autos have to
pass in the face of oncoming traffic. That would never be tolerated in
the US, where long rigs are allowed only on limited access multi-lane
roads (Interstates, freeways).

The Australian outback looks a lot like West Texas and Eastern New
Mexico, my home base, where one sees another vehicle every half-hour.
Also western Utah, where gas stations are 150 miles apart. 

Last weekend I drove in blinding snow through Charleston, WVa, where I
saw an accident involving at least three trucks with long trailers.
I-77/79 southbound was backed up more than ten miles. Later, on the
news, I learned of an 80 vehicle accident in Mercer, Pa, that blocked
the highway for 8 hours.

I remember approaching Tulsa from the east a few years ago .. at
night, in the rain, in a construction zone with guard rails close to
the roadway. I slowed down to 70 mph.Trucks, including doubles, were
passing at 80+ mph. I exited at the first motel.

>"""4. In the Tanami Desert in NT, where there is now a 400 tonne seven
>(yes seven) trailer two engine road train servicing "the Granites" gold
>mining project. I can't wait to for that one to pass me."""

Hearsay from a bicyclist. Newmont Mining says nothing about it and the
above Web site refutes the claim.
```

```
On Sun, 02 Jan 2005 11:23:52 -0500, SkippyPB
<swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

>On Sun, 02 Jan 2005 09:46:48 GMT, Robert Wagner
><spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> enlightened us:

>>That site is misinformed. The standard US triple uses 28 foot trailers
>>and has a length of 105 feet. A 98 foot triple cannot be made from
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.ai.org/dot/motoristinfo/tollroad/faq.html

The site says:

"Trailer Combinations (Triples and Long Doubles) operate under
separate rules. Contact Patron Services for information, ext. 420."

The permit application form gives those rules:

"A combination of three vehicles (tractor-trailer-trailer) whose
trailers do not exceed 48 feet, 6 inches each, the width of which
does not exceed 8 feet, 6 inches, and the height does not exceed 13
feet, 6 inches. The maximum weight, subject to axle limits,
may not exceed 127,400 pounds. CAUTION: You must contact the Indiana
Toll Road Authority to obtain a toll road certification
for this type of vehicle prior to applying for this permit.

A combination of 4 vehicles (tractor and triple trailer) whose
trailers do not exceed 28 feet, 6 inches each, the width of which
does not exceed 8 feet, 6 inches, and the height does not exceed 13
feet, 6 inches. The maximum weight, subject to axle limits,
may not exceed 127,400 pounds. CAUTION: This type of triple trailer
combination is not allowed on any highway other
than an Indiana toll road, except between an Indiana toll road
designated make-up, break-up area and the nearest toll gate."

www.in.gov/dor/mcs/pdfs-forms/m233t.pdf

Neighboring Ohio has similar rules:
http://www.ohioturnpike.org/faresch.html
```

```
>> http://www.pilotcars.com/indiana.htm

> That site is misinformed. The standard US triple uses 28 foot
trailers
> and has a length of 105 feet.

Misinformed sites now is it ?  I am sure they will give any corrections
you make to them all the consideration they deserve.

> A 98 foot triple cannot be made from readily available equipment.

65 foot double plus 28 foot trailer seem to fit within 98 feet.  But am
I _sure_ that you are more expert than, say, the  AITA.

> The triples running on I-80 (a toll road) do not have escort cars, as
the site claims they must.

I don't see anywhere on the site where they claim an escort is needed
for a 98 foot, 120,000 lb unit.  Certainly for oversize they would need
one, as they specify.

> I got the impression the mining company had paid for the road.

They may well have paid for the road, but that doesn't make it
'private'.  'Private' means no one else can use it without permission.
If it has been built on public land then anyone can.

> If the limit is 160 feet, that means Aussies are using 20 foot
trailers.

That does not mean that _all_ trailers are 20 foot.  It may mean that
ore tippers are 20 foot for _that_ road train and/or the 160 foot limit
is raised in that area.

> Hearsay from a bicyclist.

How ironic, when _all_ that you said was hearsay.  Here is a 7 tipper:
http://territorypictures.tripod.com/pages/rtrains/images/bhrtrain.jpg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Rail vs truck was Re: OT - Re: Program templates as Object Classes

_(thread depth: 39)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-03T02:38:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39bht05cnu7e9uvisnp4og4i3g4vs1e7ut@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<qcddr092lr24vvjhsei9epgf38mdqc0a69@4ax.com> <cpkak4$jqd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3prr0p482odrbaqtb2d8fenanu92ueo94@4ax.com> <cpkv39$2nh$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <t06sr0lgorg5vhq74jrnl45pimvor4a2d2@4ax.com> <33gd0eF3uqbhcU1@uni-berlin.de> <5ra6t0lu4nmd4091mt6fshiuf5qk0mfha4@4ax.com> <1104617170.404234.147860@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <c8gft09vc6vt1n2e3rnsnjhn1dmo5u9iu7@4ax.com> <1104693908.851163.66010@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 2 Jan 2005 11:25:08 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>>> http://www.pilotcars.com/indiana.htm
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>I _sure_ that you are more expert than, say, the  AITA.

That's called a Rocky Mountain Double. It's not commonly used on the 
East Coast.

>> If the limit is 160 feet, that means Aussies are using 20 foot
>trailers.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is raised in that area.

On all road trains I've seen, trailers appeared to be the same length.
I'm guessing it's 20 feet based on 160/7. 

Rigs with more than two trailers are nearly impossible to control. The
driver has one eye on the road ahead and the other on rear view mirror
watching the last trailer, which fishtails like crazy. Most drivers
are afraid to drive triples. 

It's all about money. Big rigs save money at the price of endangering
civilians and normal truckers. Their participation in accidents is
well above average based on road-miles.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Rail vs truck was Re: OT - Re: Program templates as Object Classes

_(thread depth: 38)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T14:57:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<creav3$6li$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<sqtcr0h86rgg38nee8rufhn3fevql240o7@4ax.com> <1102485864.198828.257240@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <qcddr092lr24vvjhsei9epgf38mdqc0a69@4ax.com> <cpkak4$jqd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3prr0p482odrbaqtb2d8fenanu92ueo94@4ax.com> <cpkv39$2nh$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <t06sr0lgorg5vhq74jrnl45pimvor4a2d2@4ax.com> <33gd0eF3uqbhcU1@uni-berlin.de> <5ra6t0lu4nmd4091mt6fshiuf5qk0mfha4@4ax.com> <1104617170.404234.147860@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <c8gft09vc6vt1n2e3rnsnjhn1dmo5u9iu7@4ax.com>`

```

On  2-Jan-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> The big difference is they're running on two-lane roads. Autos have to
> pass in the face of oncoming traffic. That would never be tolerated in
> the US, where long rigs are allowed only on limited access multi-lane
> roads (Interstates, freeways).

When I lived in Iowa in the 1970s, they had a law against double bottomed
trucks.   Trucks would stop at the border and split up for the drive across I-80
(or use I-70 instead.     For some reason, I have gotten in the habit of
counting wheels of trucks - 2-4-6-8-9 * 2 = 18.   Mainly I count when it looks
as though the truck has more than 18 wheels.   Surprising how many I find after
being introduced to the term 18-wheeler back in CB days.   But you never hear of
someone driving a 22-wheeler for some reason.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Rail vs truck was Re: OT - Re: Program templates as Object Classes

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T20:28:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104377556.375420.268880@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5ra6t0lu4nmd4091mt6fshiuf5qk0mfha4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ujdcr0lp07f32k2tet2kinchs3gs321ar4@4ax.com> <1102474692.144778.157460@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <sqtcr0h86rgg38nee8rufhn3fevql240o7@4ax.com> <1102485864.198828.257240@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <qcddr092lr24vvjhsei9epgf38mdqc0a69@4ax.com> <cpkak4$jqd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3prr0p482odrbaqtb2d8fenanu92ueo94@4ax.com> <cpkv39$2nh$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <t06sr0lgorg5vhq74jrnl45pimvor4a2d2@4ax.com> <33gd0eF3uqbhcU1@uni-berlin.de> <5ra6t0lu4nmd4091mt6fshiuf5qk0mfha4@4ax.com>`

```
> Someone mentioned monster seven-trailer trucks traveling the
> Australian outback at 70 mph. No so.They're called Road Trains and
> most have two or three trailers.

http://territorypictures.tripod.com/pages/rtrains/images/bhrtrain.jpg

> We have the same in northern Indiana along I-80.

Most roading system have limits on total length, total weight, and axle
weight.  The Northern Territory has much larger limits than anywhere
else or none at all.  This means that large truck-trailer combinations
(called Road Trains) can exist in NT.

For example in USA:
"""The maximum weight of a tractor-trailer is supposed to be 80,000
pounds on the interstate system. """

"""H.R. 3132 will freeze maximum trucktrailer length at 53 feet on the
NHS."""

see http://www.aitaonline.com/Weight%20Size%20Limits.html

Which shows that triples are only allowed on toll highways in Indiana.

While a double is limited to 65 foot and a triple may get to 95 foot,
the NT Road Trains run to 160 foot for a triple and can near double
that for a 7 trailer.  Weights go to 300,000 lbs or more.

They are nowhere near 'the same' in Indiana.

> The really big Australian trucks, with 6-7 trailers, travel on
private roads no faster than 40 mph.

Where do you get this nonsense about 'private roads' ?  It seems that
in the USA the triples can only run on toll roads, but in NT there are
no such thing.

Certainly the heavier the road train the more power is needed to move
it, and thus for the same power the heavier is slower, but they still
do Darwin to SA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Rail vs truck was Re: OT - Re: Program templates as Object Classes

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-03T17:28:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d9uit0l7kd0nvva9vnbq9cgcv9p7a92o78@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<sqtcr0h86rgg38nee8rufhn3fevql240o7@4ax.com> <1102485864.198828.257240@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <qcddr092lr24vvjhsei9epgf38mdqc0a69@4ax.com> <cpkak4$jqd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3prr0p482odrbaqtb2d8fenanu92ueo94@4ax.com> <cpkv39$2nh$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <t06sr0lgorg5vhq74jrnl45pimvor4a2d2@4ax.com> <33gd0eF3uqbhcU1@uni-berlin.de> <5ra6t0lu4nmd4091mt6fshiuf5qk0mfha4@4ax.com> <1104377556.375420.268880@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 2 Jan 2005 20:28:23 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Someone mentioned monster seven-trailer trucks traveling the
>> Australian outback at 70 mph. No so.They're called Road Trains and
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>pounds on the interstate system. """

More important is weight per axle. Roads are designed for max 20,000
lbs. The 80,000 figure is based on the assumption there will be four
heavy axles. Doubles and triples spread the load over more than four.

>"""H.R. 3132 will freeze maximum trucktrailer length at 53 feet on the
>NHS."""

Something's wrong here. A normal semi is 60 feet long.

>see http://www.aitaonline.com/Weight%20Size%20Limits.html
>
>Which shows that triples are only allowed on toll highways in Indiana.
>
>While a double is limited to 65 foot and a triple may get to 95 foot,

The Indiana rules for doubles and triples do not specify maximum
length for the vehicle, only for trailers. In practice, a triple is
105 feet and a long double is 110 feet.

www.in.gov/dor/mcs/pdfs-forms/m233t.pdf

>the NT Road Trains run to 160 foot for a triple and can near double
>that for a 7 trailer.  Weights go to 300,000 lbs or more.

Guinness shows the longest truck to be 4053 feet long with 87
trailers. That comes to 47 feet per, which is similar to the standard
US length of 48 feet. So you're right, an NT triple is 160 feet.

>They are nowhere near 'the same' in Indiana.

You're right.

>> The really big Australian trucks, with 6-7 trailers, travel on
>private roads no faster than 40 mph.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>no such thing.

It's about lane width. Long trucks require 12 foot lanes because the
rear trailers drift side to side. The traditional width of public
roads in the US is 8-10 feet, which is too narrow. 



>
>Certainly the heavier the road train the more power is needed to move
>it, and thus for the same power the heavier is slower, but they still
>do Darwin to SA.
```

```
the tractor is in front of that.
```

```
> On 2 Jan 2005 20:28:23 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> US length of 48 feet. So you're right, an NT triple is 160 feet.

Where in Australia did this truck run and how?  What is the normal 
maximum and what road restrictions are there?
> 
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> roads in the US is 8-10 feet, which is too narrow. 

Standard freeway lane width in the United States and Canada is 12 feet 
with a maximum vehicle width of 8 feet six inches.  Interstate buses in 
the United States used to be restricted to 8 feet wide.
```

```
Proper configuration of a Windows machine is to *NOT* use Micro$**t
software for Internet interface. None of our systems seem to have these
problems, but then we are using NT 4.0 with IE not installed (NT 4.0
didn't have IE as part of the O/S when it first came out!).
Later,
Steve.T
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T22:20:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2K3Cd.832$Vt5.234@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cnfft0l9s3tqqa12iol6q3d3eu2bfqa10j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <10rutk12o9huta7@news.supernews.com> <1103070057.930458.91050@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <10s0gka4bog6j4d@news.supernews.com> <1104517308.836203.127480@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1104638469.074444.202110@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <cnfft0l9s3tqqa12iol6q3d3eu2bfqa10j@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> On 1 Jan 2005 20:01:09 -0800, "steve.t" <sthompson@ix.netcom.com>
> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mozilla in place of IE.

We get approved patches for Windows (that, at that point, become 
"directives" for us to load, lest our access to the network be cut) at 
the pace of about one a week, some weeks more.  Like you say, most have 
to do with internetworking vulnerabilities.  There is the occasional one 
that is a vulnerability in the underlying networking software - but, if 
you've got a decent firewall, those are pretty easily avoided.

I've got responsibility for 3 W2K Server boxes and one WXP Pro box 
(actually the easiest one, as it's in the "yellow zone" (isolated from 
the base network) - it could probably become a zombie and they probably 
wouldn't even notice).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linux and security

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-01T18:02:01-05:00

- **References:** `<oj6ur01ec8isqffk4lkq2grsngnc2ra5u6@4ax.com> <10rutk12o9huta7@news.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-77FC2B.19324614122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <uiLAd.3805$Kw1.373@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-EADCC4.21542430122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <lokBd.248$4G1.39@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <lokBd.248$4G1.39@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
 LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > In article <uiLAd.3805$Kw1.373@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> a couple of us that are military in our shop, and we're all enlisted.  :)

I was just thinking about my one, temporary, stationing on an Air Force 
base.  I was in Montgomery, Alabama for Basic Noncommissioned Officers 
Course (BNCOC).  Why the army put us there, I have no idea.  But that is 
where they found a classroom and an Army instructor.

So I'm an E-5 in transient housing -- in the Army that would mean a 
rack, wall locker and bathroom shared with two to four other transients.

In the Air Force that turned out to be a suite with full kitchen, 
washer/dryer, cable TV w/VCR.  To top it off there was a mini-bar and  
maid service was available.

After that I knew why people called the Air Force a 'country club' ... 
thus the caddys and martinis.
```

```
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

[snip]

>So I'm an E-5 in transient housing -- in the Army that would mean a 
>rack, wall locker and bathroom shared with two to four other transients.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>thus the caddys and martinis.

When I enlisted the Air Force had the shortest period of Basic Training, a 
guaranteed tech-school placement program (if you did not get the schooling 
you signed up for your enlistment contract was null and void) and a 
reputation - well deserved - for the best chow, overall, of any of the 
services.

I ran across people who were proud of volunteering for situations that 
made them more uncomfortable and derided me for the 'soft life' I had... 
my response was 'Hey, if I want to be miserable I can always get boots a 
half-size smaller; if you want to sleep on the ground instead of in a bunk 
then be my guest.'

DD
```

```
If you came from Atlanta, Maxwell is the second one.  I don't know 
exactly where you stayed, but I've got a pretty good idea...

> It was some time ago -- that was 1995 or 1996...

It just doesn't seem like that should be that long ago, does it?  This 
is now 2005 - Top Gun was released in 1986, meaning that next year, that 
will be *20 years ago*!

> My father, a 42-year veteran of the Army, used to always tell us kids to 
> 'join the Air Force'.  I just never knew how to listen...

One of the guys that used to be in our office had enlisted with the 
Army, been sent to 'Nam and made it back - he actually transferred to 
the Air Force.  He ended up retiring at E-7 - he was sharp enough for 
E-9, but he just didn't want to go through the political B$ that he 
would have had to endure for those years.  :)
```

## Thread: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits — 1 this month / 1132 total

_Thread started [2004-10](2004-10.md#t-4e5f66a214)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Kidnapped and sent to another country was Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 50)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-01-01T19:21:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<33opm5F4232lqU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<kyEsd.137$l%5.34958@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com> <31feaaF3atelrU1@individual.net> <9MIqSSZ9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <31gcp0F39v7qvU1@individual.net> <kyEsd.137$l%5.34958@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> to Saudia Arabia by american secret police.

Assuming that you are referring to the Mahar Arar (spelling not 
guaranteed) case, it is very troubling.  For the rest of you who haven't 
heard, Mahar is a naturalized Canadian citizen who was born in Syria. 
On the way back from somewhere in the Muslim world, he was transiting 
through New York enroute to Canada (presumably Ottawa or Montreal). 
Customs and Immigration decided he was a threat of some sort of a threat 
possibly based on information from the Royal Canadian Mounted Police 
(equivalent of the FBI for these purposes) and/or the Canadian Security 
and Intelligence Service (CSIS which I believe expands to what I wrote). 
  They decided to ship him to Syria where he was tortured and it took 
the press and work by a number of Members of Parliament to get him 
released.  There have been leaks purporting to show his ties to 
terrorism and there is a special inquiry looking into this.  In my not 
so humble opinion, even assuming he is a terrorist which is vehemently 
denied this practice is disastrous for both Canadian and US citizens 
especially those born in other countries.  It sets the dangerous 
precedent that you can be sent to a third country any time you incur the 
suspicion of an immigration agent and your country's security 
establishment if you are passing through an airport in a country 
friendly to your country's intelligence service.  It is especially 
ironic given the terrorism label given Syria by the US.
> 
> As someone that live 150 miles from the border that concerns me somewhat.
> 
> Donald
>
```

<a id="t-dca40d9c75"></a>
## Thread: utility to read cobol data files? — 3 this month / 10 total

_Thread started [2004-12](2004-12.md#t-dca40d9c75)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: utility to read cobol data files?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T22:00:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<33rnckF40ke5eU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<MZ3wd.526767$%k.136426@pd7tw2no>`
- **References:** `<cpp5fu$om4$1@fegnews.vip.hr> <btYvd.5851$%s7.1335@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com> <B03wd.222$iC4.158@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com> <MZ3wd.526767$%k.136426@pd7tw2no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> Tom Morrison wrote:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> from what has been related to me.

On the mainframes where an indexed file was a part of the operating 
environment, provision of a COBOL-specific indexed file would have been 
counter-productive.  VSAM KSDS (key sequenced data sets) are available 
to all of the languages and a host of utilities.  I am fairly certain 
the same is true of Unisys and its predecessors.  I have no idea as to 
what the situation is for the various contingents (DEC, Tandem, etc.) of 
HP.  If Microfocus or other vendors had developed a compiler for the 
environments I mentioned they would have used the existing file system 
just as Dylakor and Easytrieve did with their 4th generation (report 
writing) languages.
> 
> Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: utility to read cobol data files?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T18:13:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104718419.834884.115440@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<33rnckF40ke5eU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cpp5fu$om4$1@fegnews.vip.hr> <btYvd.5851$%s7.1335@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com> <B03wd.222$iC4.158@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com> <MZ3wd.526767$%k.136426@pd7tw2no> <33rnckF40ke5eU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
> If Microfocus or other vendors had developed a compiler for the
> environments I mentioned they would have used the existing file
system

When MicroFocus CIS Cobol was developed for the ICL DRS20 system they
did indeed using the existing DRX File System for Indexed and Relative
(DSAM) files.  These could then be shared with the other languages on
that system.
For Unix MF adopted C-ISAM which was defined in the X-Open standard.
```

<a id="t-b0dbb19da6"></a>
## Thread: Status 39 — 1 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [2004-12](2004-12.md#t-b0dbb19da6)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Status 39

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-02T19:07:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103739897.649954.243030@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<41c964f2$0$1828$a729d347@news.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<41c80bb3$0$1848$a729d347@news.telepac.pt> <10sgd0vhou9shb7@news.supernews.com> <41c964f2$0$1828$a729d347@news.telepac.pt>`

```
> I'm thinking it could be because of the SP2 from Fujitsu. I installed
it
> in my computer and in the customer computers. I've already compile
the
> program in a computer that doesn't have the SP2 installed but the
problem
> continues.

When you say 'SP2 from Fujitsu', I assume that you mean 'UP2' as in:

""" --------------------------------
Fujitsu NetCOBOL for Windows Version 7 Update Pack 2 - Development
last updated on 11/16/04,8044KB

This is Update Pack 2 for the development version of NetCOBOL for
Windows Version 7. This update includes:

* Support for Windows XP SP2
* All of the fixes in NetCOBOL Update Pack 1
* Misc. other fixes

Installation instructions and a list of Development fixes can be found
<a
href="http://www.netcobol.com/coboldlp/FC-NET/Software/Readme_NetCOBOLV7_UP2.htm">here</a>,
and are provided in readme files included in the download .zip file.
For more information about how Windows XP SP2 affects NetCOBOL please
see this <a
href="http://www.netcobol.com/coboldlp/FC-NET/Software/Readme_Windows_XP_SP2.htm">readme
file</a>, also found in the download .zip file.
---------------------------------------""""

The problem is most likely yet another effect of Windows XP SP2 that
Fujitsu hasn't discovered yet, so you should report this to Fujitsu
support and in the meantime revert to whatever works, which is probably
XP less SP2 and less Fujitsu UP2.
```

```
Yes, guess who has to be very careful about things so that there is no
system crash. And if you are 1099, at $25/hr you probably can't afford
E&O insurance.

But you don't have to crash the system, you can just make a simple
mistake and corrupt data, or cause payroll to *not* produce certain
checks (boy did I ever have a headache with that one at Denny's oh so
many years ago -- "No we don't use this system to produce Canadian
payroll" them.  Me, "Are you absolutely sure, because I keep seeing
statements in the comments about needing this or that for Canada."
Them, "Yes, bypass that code or remove it and put this into
production." Betcha can guess what didn't get produced the following
week after I was gone!).

Later,
Steve.T
```

```
Hello Everyone,

my character-base cobol program? I want to run my cobol program
under the control of the graphical window, rather than the default
console window that gets created by the cobol runtime system.
i am using Net Express 3.1 under windows 2000 professional.
thanks for the kind help and feedback, Kellie.
```

```
generate a DOS Window.
```

- **References:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d_CCd.694252$nl.175415@pd7tw3no> <1104877007.115424.185430@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
As a matter of protocol the '>' characters are used to indicate a quote
from someone else. You seem to be putting a '>' at the start of lines
that you type (or your software is doing this) which makes it look like
what you have written as being from someone else.

It should look like this:
--------------------------------
> stuff you are reply to.

Your reply.


Wheras what appears is:
---------------------------------
> stuff you are replying to
> your reply which looks like stuff you are replying to
```

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T18:05:09-08:00

- **References:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d_CCd.694252$nl.175415@pd7tw3no> <1104877007.115424.185430@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
> I would like to run my character-base program inside the GUI window
> without creating another console window (dos-window).

It will still look exactly the same as the console window.

> In other words,
> my program will be POUCHED inside the parent Window (GUI), and I have
> no need to display another dos window.

I have no idea why you use the word 'pouched' or what you impy by this.
Perhaps you want an 80 x 25 area within another GUI window that is
controlled by something else so that you get something like a text
control with your program running in it and other controls are on the
same window.

I can't think of any reason why you would want to do that, nor can I
think of any way that Windows would allow that to happen with a cosole
program.

> There is a company named WRQ.COM
> on the internet, that have been applying this approach for IBM
mainframe
> cobol programs in a system called reflection.

Reflection is a terminal emulator for Web and Windows.  It is not
displaying a Windows cosole or DOS program running locally in its
window it is displaying remote text data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can coBol imitates a kangaroo?...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T20:13:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104898416.720750.115540@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1104895398.717387.183000@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d_CCd.694252$nl.175415@pd7tw3no> <1104877007.115424.185430@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1104889797.319201.146910@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1104895398.717387.183000@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
> well, a graphical window will give me more control by
> knowing that the user have clicked the "x" button,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> name, programmed by Queen Kellie. :---)

So, you don't want to have your existing text mode screen within a GUI
screen you want your program to operate a GUI screen.

You can't do that with MF Cobol doing ACCEPT/DISPLAY or Screen SECTION.

(Actually you _can_ do that using ACCEPT/DISPLAY if you had ACCU COBOL,
but it still needs quite a bit of rewriting)

You would have to rewrite the program to use Dialog or SP/2 or Windows
API if you really want a proper GUI.  I have done these.  SP/2 is
relatively easy as you draw what the screens look like, ie where all
the buttons go, where the fields are, what the menu has.  The program
then sets up the field values that are to be displayed and CALLs "SP2"
USING Panel-Area ...  When the CALL returns there are values that tell
you what the user has done.  You can get a demo from flexus.com.

Dialog I have not used.  Windows API is complicated and needs some
months to be learnt.

You _can_ simulate a GUI using ACCEPT/DISPLAY.  The CURSOR IS .. allows
you to control the position of the text cursor and will tell you where
it was when the user returns.

The mouse can be used with ADIS as you can make mouse clicks return to
the program with the position.  You can display a 'sort of' button
using double and single line draw characters or just -|+ (you need to
use fixed font here):

+-------+
| START |
+=======+

You need to check for these after _every_ ACCEPT.

You can simulate a menu too, but it all rewrite and you would be much
better to just get SP/2 as you can use this on different Cobol systems,
different operating systems and even use it over the internet.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can coBol imitates a kangaroo?...

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T13:55:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1104875741.006022.59540@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
> I want to run my cobol program under the control of the
> graphical window, rather than the default
> console window that gets created by the cobol runtime system.

You can write your program to CALL Windows API routines to create
graphical windows, dialog boxes and controls and to have callback
routines to handle user interaction.  ie it can be a full GUI program
written in COBOL.  You can even use the drawing routines in the API to
draw graphs or pictures, or anything.

You could also use a GUI interface such as MF's Dialog or Flexus SP/2
to more easily create the user interface and handle the grubby MS
Windows (or other) API.  The program would still need to CALL the
interface handler.

If you want to find something that will take an existing program that
uses ACCEPT/DISPLAY and automatically transform it into a GUI program
with drop-down boxes and push buttons, then no, there  is unlikely to
be anything that would do that sensibly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can coBol imitates a kangaroo?...

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-05T07:23:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-4C2B0D.07230305012005@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
 KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM wrote:

> Hello Everyone,
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks for the kind help and feedback, Kellie.

I've not done this (or anything) with Net Express.  But I have done this 
with other languages.  It requires knowledge of the "CreateProcess" (or 
maybe "ShellExecute") WinAPI call.  You are allowed to pass descriptors 
for the three standard file streams, sysin, sysout and syserr.

Write the program like any normal DOS application, with ACCEPT and 
DISPLAY.  Expect it to run under a CMD.com shell.  When you do your 
CreateProcess call, you might be able to get away with just calling your 
application.  But depending upon the Net Express runtime and all, you 
might have to invoke a CMD.com shell and tell that to call your 
application.  Your command string is going to look like one of these two:

   "myprog.exe arg1 arg2 ... argn"

   or 

   "cmd /k myprog.exe arg1 arg2 ... argn" (the /k flag keeps the cmd 
window hidden)

So set up your GUI application with whatever text window you want to 
have and populate it with the characters coming in from the programs 
sysout stream.  Send your syserr stream to the same place and save 
yourself 1/3 of the trouble.

Send input by writing to your sysin stream.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can coBol imitates a kangaroo?...

- **From:** "MrJohn" <john.oman@modisit.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-07T07:59:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1105113594.061391.113500@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1104940100.903446.190420@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1104856384.588106.268190@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <joe_zitzelberger-4C2B0D.07230305012005@knology.usenetserver.com> <1104940100.903446.190420@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
I read your thread with interest.  What you are asking is difficult at
best.  Excuse me while I tell you how our company solved has dealt with
this.

We built a product that is called XConsole that does what you are
asking.  You run a console program that displays a GUI screen (like a
terminal emulator).  Then you run "catch" program that runs the client
programs.  This catch program intercepts all DISPLAY and ACCEPT verbs
and redirects them to the console program.  From the console program,
you can display all active programs, waiting programs and messages.
This console is like a hub that can "watch" all programs for messages
and respond to them.

As you can see, we went to great lengths to solve your problem, and
some others.

John Oman
john.oman@modisit.com
Dir of Development
Modis Solutions
(center for COBOL excellance)
```

## Thread: COBOL — 1 this month / 19 total

_Thread started [2004-12](2004-12.md#t-dabd1470ea)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-01-04T11:04:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crepc1$1tc5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<781a5bf97064111bb1fb057deb0cb1a0@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com> <cqet70$1v83$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <331orsF3sn49mU1@individual.net> <9NT5F9LPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9NT5F9LPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  24.12.04
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> punched tape.

Actually, B6500 COBOL(60) had CARD-READER with READER as a synonym; it also
had PAPER-TAPE-READER.  Paper tape devices were apparently dropped very
early in the history of the B6700; COBOL(68) has no SELECT syntax for them,
though it continued to allow CARD-READER and CARD-PUNCH.  By the time
COBOL74 rolled around the SELECTable devices had been pared down to just
seven (DISK, TAPE, READER, PUNCH, PRINTER, REMOTE and PORT)  with any and
all refinements beyond that handled programmatically either by VALUE OF
extensions in the FD or CHANGE ATTRIBUTE statements in the Procedure
DIVISION, or externally by WFL file equations, at compile time, at execution
time, or through WFL MODIFY statements against the object code file itself.

For sequential files, there are only a few edge cases in which the object
code for the I/O statements differs anyway; the system's had almost entirely
device-independent I/O for a very, very long time.  A program with a READER
file will operate just fine if you reassign it to an extant file on TAPE or
DISK, or even an available REMOTE or PORT file.  If you try something
inappropriate like attempt to OPEN a file INPUT when it's actually assigned
to a printer or printer-backup device, or OUTPUT when it's actually assigned
to READER (which these days is actually a file in the WFL stream; we no
longer support physical card devices), most likely you'll get your knuckles
rapped at execution time.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

---

```
Israel/Occupied Territories: Excessive use of force
Amnesty International is concerned about the deterioration of the human
rights and humanitarian situation as a result of the Israeli army
incursion in the Jabaliya refugee camp and surrounding areas in the

Hanoun and Beit Lahiya).

In the past week (since the evening of Tuesday 28 September) more than
70 Palestinians have been killed, more than a third of whom were
unarmed and including some 20 children. Hundreds of others have been
injured.

The Israeli army has repeatedly used excessive force, including heavy
shelling from tanks and helicopter gunships. Experience has shown that

by the army in densely populated residential areas (the Jabaliya
refugee camp is one of the most densely populated places in the world,
with more than 100,000 Palestinians living in less than two square
kilometres), invariably results in a high rate of death and injury of
bystanders and people who are not involved in armed confrontation.
```
